# rhodeislandbankruptcy.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on rhodeislandbankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## Rhode Island Bankruptcy -- Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Rhode Island Bankruptcy -- Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Rhode Island has a moderate filing rate. Despite being the smallest state, the district handles a significant caseload relative to population. This free guide covers everything Rhode Island residents need to know about filing bankruptcy, from exemptions to costs to what happens after your case is filed.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

$500,000

Homestead Exemption

1

Federal District

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 -- Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 -- Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Rhode Island Federal Court District

Rhode Island has 1 federal bankruptcy district. Where you live determines where you file.

District of Rhode Island (D.R.I.)

over 25,000 cases

Courthouse: 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903

Phone: (401) 626-3100 | rib.uscourts.gov

Rhode Island Exemptions at a Glance

Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your primary residence. This is one of the most generous homestead exemptions in the country.

PropertyRhode Island Exemption

Homestead$500,000

Vehicle$12,000

Household goods$8,600 total

WildcardNo general wildcard

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Rhode Island?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,200 to $2,200 for Chapter 7; $3,000 to $5,000 for Chapter 13 in Rhode Island

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Rhode Island?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Rhode Island typically range from $1,200 to $2,200 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You will also need to pay for two required credit counseling courses, usually $25-$50 total. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments through your repayment plan. See all exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan before receiving a discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and type of debt determine which chapter is best. Compare chapters.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island has 1 federal bankruptcy district: D.R.I.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

See all frequently asked questions →

Need Free Bankruptcy Help?

If you cannot afford a bankruptcy attorney, free help is available. Legal aid organizations, law school clinics, and court self-help programs can assist you at no cost.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Rhode Island -- Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Rhode Island residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors -- but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test in Rhode Island

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the Rhode Island median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility

For a single filer in Rhode Island, the current median is approximately $55,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Rhode Island Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep.

PropertyRhode Island Exemption

Homestead$500,000

Vehicle$12,000

Household goods$8,600 total

WildcardNo general wildcard

Tools of trade$2,000

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation -- Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing -- Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately -- creditors must stop all collection activity.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting -- Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education -- Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge -- The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts incurred through fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7 in Rhode Island?

The means test compares your household income to the Rhode Island median. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify. If above, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan instead. For a single filer in Rhode Island, the current median is approximately $55,000/year.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting of creditors occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island exemptions protect: homestead equity up to $500,000, vehicle equity up to $12,000, household goods up to $8,600 total, and retirement accounts are fully exempt. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Rhode Island -- Payment Plans, Timeline
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Understanding the process and your Rhode Island exemptions is critical to success.

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

$500,000

Homestead Exemption

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income -- the difference between your income and reasonable living expenses. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills -- often pennies on the dollar

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the Rhode Island median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts. You keep your home, car, and other property.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 -- Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors. The trustee reviews your plan and finances.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Chapter 13 plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the Rhode Island median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above the median, your plan must be 5 years. During this time, you make monthly payments to a trustee who distributes the money to your creditors.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. One of the main benefits of Chapter 13 is the ability to keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your repayment plan. The automatic stay stops foreclosure proceedings immediately upon filing. Rhode Island's homestead exemption of $500,000 also applies.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

If your Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the automatic stay ends and creditors can resume collection activity. You lose the protection of the bankruptcy court. Any payments you made through the plan were distributed to creditors, but you do not receive a discharge of your remaining debts. Learn more.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Rhode Island Bankruptcy Exemptions -- What You Can Keep
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding Rhode Island's exemptions is critical -- they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Rhode Island requires filers to use state exemptions -- you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

Rhode Island State Exemptions -- Full List

Homestead -- $500,000

Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your primary residence. This is one of the most generous homestead exemptions in the country.

R.I. Gen. Laws 9-26-4.1

Motor Vehicle -- $12,000

Protects equity in one motor vehicle up to the exemption limit. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

Other Rhode Island Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Household goods, furnishings$8,600 total

ClothingAll necessary clothing exempt

Tools of trade$2,000

Jewelry$2,000

WildcardNo general wildcard

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Public assistance benefitsFully exempt

Special Notes

Rhode Island requires filers to use state exemptions. The $500,000 homestead exemption provides strong protection for homeowners.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Rhode Island homestead exemption?

Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your primary residence. This is one of the most generous homestead exemptions in the country.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island's motor vehicle exemption is $12,000. If your car equity is within that limit, you keep it in Chapter 7.

Does Rhode Island allow federal exemptions?

No. Rhode Island requires filers to use state exemptions only.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Rhode Island? -- Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/cost.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Rhode Island?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,200 to $2,200Typical Rhode Island range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free from annualcreditreport.com

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$3,000 to $5,000Typical Rhode Island range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

Attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through your monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Rhode Island Legal Services -- Free legal services for low-income Rhode Islanders, including bankruptcy.

Fee waiver -- eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments -- pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing -- filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended for most filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Rhode Island?

The Chapter 7 filing fee is $338. Attorney fees in Rhode Island typically range from $1,200 to $2,200. Credit counseling courses cost about $25-50 total (two required). Fee waivers are available if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Rhode Island?

The Chapter 13 filing fee is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $3,000 to $5,000 in Rhode Island. The filing fee and attorney fees can be paid through your repayment plan, so you usually do not need to pay everything upfront.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The court filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Legal aid organizations in Rhode Island may provide free or reduced-cost legal representation for qualifying individuals.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, attorney fees are typically included in your repayment plan, so you may only need a small upfront payment.

## Rhode Island Bankruptcy FAQ -- 15 Common Questions Answered
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/faq.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Rhode Island Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the 15 most common questions from Rhode Island residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,200 to $2,200 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Chapter 13 fees can be paid through your repayment plan. Fee waivers available for low-income Chapter 7 filers.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your home. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan. Most homeowners keep their homes in bankruptcy.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island protects $12,000 in vehicle equity. If your car equity is within the exemption limit, you keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments through your plan.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect the moment your petition is filed and immediately stops wage garnishments, bank levies, lawsuits, foreclosures, and repossessions.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and eliminates most unsecured debt, but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep all property and catch up on secured debt arrears, but requires 3-5 years of payments. Your income, assets, and goals determine the best choice.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island has 1 district: D.R.I.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Yes. Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years and Chapter 13 for 7 years. However, many filers see credit scores start recovering within 1-2 years as debt-to-income ratios improve.

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are unsecured debts that are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. Medical debt is one of the most common reasons people file bankruptcy.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no. Student loans are not dischargeable except in cases of undue hardship, which requires a separate adversary proceeding and a difficult legal standard.

What is the means test?

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. It compares your household income to the Rhode Island median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation determines eligibility.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity, including phone calls, letters, and emails. Creditors who violate the stay can be held in contempt and ordered to pay damages.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. After a Chapter 7 discharge, you must wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After a Chapter 13 discharge, wait 6 years for Chapter 7 or 2 years for another Chapter 13.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay is a court order that takes effect immediately when you file bankruptcy. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Rhode Island Bankruptcy Statistics -- Filing Data and Trends
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/statistics.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008-2024). Rhode Island has a moderate filing rate. Despite being the smallest state, the district handles a significant caseload relative to population.

1

Federal District

$500,000

Homestead Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Rhode Island Federal Court District

DistrictCases (2008-2024)Primary Location

District of Rhode Island (D.R.I.)over 25,000Providence

Key Facts About Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Homestead exemption: $500,000 -- Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your primary residence.

Chapter 13 dismissal risk: Nationally, about one-third to two-fifths of Chapter 13 cases end in dismissal. Quality of legal representation is one of the strongest predictors of plan completion.

Prior filer rate: Across the nation, roughly 25-30% of Chapter 13 filers had a prior bankruptcy case, suggesting many debtors cycle through the system without achieving lasting relief.

Means test: Rhode Island's median income for a single filer is approximately $55,000/year.

Understanding the Data

Chapter 13 plans run 3-5 years. Cases filed in recent years (2020-2024) are still active if succeeding -- only early dismissals have been counted. The most reliable dismissal rates come from mature years (2008-2019) where most cases have reached final outcomes.

Source: Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database, 2008-2024. Analysis by Open Bankruptcy Project.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many bankruptcy cases are filed in Rhode Island?

Since 2008, Rhode Island's district has processed over 25,000+ bankruptcy cases. Filing volumes peaked around 2010-2011 following the financial crisis and have generally declined since.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate?

Nationally, about 33-40% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before completion. Rates vary by district and depend on factors including the local legal market, judicial practices, and the complexity of cases filed. National dismissal rate data.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Rhode Island Bankruptcy Resources -- Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/resources.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process in Rhode Island.

Bankruptcy Courts

District of Rhode Island

Address: 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903

Phone: (401) 626-3100

Website: rib.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Rhode Island Legal Services

Free legal services for low-income Rhode Islanders, including bankruptcy.

Phone: (401) 274-2652

Website: rils.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required for bankruptcy: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov -- approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 1: (617) 788-0400

Website: justice.gov/ust

Means Test Explained

How to File Bankruptcy

Case Dismissed?

File Again?

Non-Dischargeable Debts

Dismissal Rate Data

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the bankruptcy court in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island has 1 federal bankruptcy court. D.R.I.: 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903 ((401) 626-3100)

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. Rhode Island Legal Services ((401) 274-2652) provides free legal services.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list of approved credit counseling agencies at justice.gov/ust. Most offer courses online or by phone for $15-25.

## Filing Bankruptcy in Providence, Rhode Island -- Your Complete Guide
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/

Providence Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Filing Bankruptcy in Providence, Rhode Island

Free 2026 guide to bankruptcy in Providence. Covers Chapter 7, Chapter 13, Rhode Island exemptions, costs, and what to expect when filing in the D.R.I..

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

$500,000

Homestead Exemption

$12,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Explore Providence Bankruptcy Topics

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy

Learn how Chapter 7 works in Providence. Wipe out most unsecured debt in 3-4 months.

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy

Repay debts over 3-5 years while keeping your property. How Chapter 13 works in Providence.

Rhode Island Exemptions

What you can keep: homestead ($500,000), vehicle ($12,000), and more.

Cost of Filing

Filing fees, attorney costs, and credit counseling fees for Providence residents.

Frequently Asked Questions

Common questions about filing bankruptcy in Providence, Rhode Island.

Means Test

Do you qualify for Chapter 7? Rhode Island median income thresholds and the means test calculation.

341 Meeting of Creditors

What to expect at the 341 meeting in Providence. Location, preparation, and common questions.

Courthouse Information

District: D.R.I.

Courthouse: 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903

Legal Aid: Rhode Island Legal Services

You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Nearby Cities

Bankruptcy information for communities near Providence, Rhode Island.

Warwick

Kent County · Pop. 82,000

Cranston

Providence County · Pop. 82,000

Pawtucket

Providence County · Pop. 75,000

East Providence

Providence County · Pop. 48,000

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Providence, Rhode Island?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Providence typically range from $1,200 to $2,200 for Chapter 7 and $3,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Providence?

Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you keep your home while catching up on missed payments.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Providence?

Bankruptcy cases in Providence are filed in the D.R.I.. The courthouse is located at 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903.

How long does bankruptcy take in Providence?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan before receiving a discharge.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Providence, Rhode Island -- Eliminate Debt
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/chapter-7.html

Providence Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Providence, Rhode Island

Chapter 7 is the fastest form of bankruptcy relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is how it works for Providence residents filing in the D.R.I..

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 bankruptcy (also called "liquidation" bankruptcy) eliminates most unsecured debts -- credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. In exchange, non-exempt assets may be sold to pay creditors. However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In Rhode Island, you can protect up to $500,000 in home equity and $12,000 in vehicle equity. Additional exemptions cover household goods, clothing, retirement accounts, and more.

Do You Qualify? The Means Test

To file Chapter 7 in Rhode Island, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the Rhode Island median.

Household SizeAnnual Median Income

1 person$63,147

2 persons$80,655

3 persons$97,449

4 persons$119,467

If your income is below the median for your household size, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation may still allow you to file.

Chapter 7 Timeline in Providence

1File petition with the D.R.I.

Your attorney files your petition, schedules, and means test. The automatic stay immediately stops collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

2341 Meeting of Creditors (30 days)

You attend a brief meeting of creditors where the trustee asks questions under oath about your finances.

3Discharge (60-90 days after 341)

If no objections are filed, you receive your discharge order eliminating qualifying debts.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans

Old utility bills

Most old tax debts

Deficiency balances

Not Dischargeable

Student loans (usually)

Child support / alimony

Recent tax debts

Debts from fraud

DUI injury debts

Government fines

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I qualify for Chapter 7 in Providence?

You must pass the means test. If your household income is below the Rhode Island median, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation determines eligibility.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Providence?

Typically 3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors is usually 30 days after filing.

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not eliminate student loans (usually), child support, alimony, most tax debts, or debts from fraud.

Will I lose my car in Chapter 7 in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island's vehicle exemption protects up to $12,000 of equity in your car. If your car equity is below that amount, you keep it.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Providence, Rhode Island -- Repayment Plans
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/chapter-13.html

Providence Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Providence, Rhode Island

Chapter 13 lets you reorganize your debts into a manageable 3-5 year repayment plan while keeping your property. Here is how it works for Providence residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Chapter 13 Works

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not eliminate debts immediately. Instead, you propose a repayment plan to pay back some or all of your debts over 3-5 years. Key benefits:

Keep your home -- catch up on missed mortgage payments through the plan

Keep your car -- restructure car loan payments, potentially at a lower interest rate

No means test -- income determines plan length, not eligibility

Protect co-signers -- the co-debtor stay prevents creditors from pursuing co-signers

Cram down car loans -- if your car loan is older than 910 days, you may reduce the balance to the car's value

Plan Length and Payments

Your plan length depends on your income relative to the Rhode Island median:

Income LevelPlan LengthPayment Basis

Below Rhode Island median3 yearsDisposable income after allowed expenses

Above Rhode Island median5 yearsDisposable income after allowed expenses

Your monthly payment is based on your disposable income -- the amount left after subtracting necessary living expenses from your income. Unsecured creditors receive whatever your disposable income can pay over the plan period.

Chapter 13 Timeline in Providence

1File petition and proposed plan

Your attorney files your petition and proposed repayment plan with the D.R.I.. The automatic stay stops all collection actions.

2Begin plan payments (30 days)

You must begin making plan payments within 30 days of filing, even before the plan is confirmed.

3341 Meeting of Creditors (30-45 days)

Brief meeting where the trustee reviews your finances and plan.

4Confirmation hearing (2-3 months)

The court approves or modifies your plan. Creditors may object.

5Complete plan payments (3-5 years)

Make all plan payments through the trustee. Remaining qualifying unsecured debts are discharged.

Frequently Asked Questions

How does Chapter 13 work in Providence?

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years through a court-approved plan. Cases are filed in the D.R.I..

How long is a Chapter 13 plan?

If your income is below the Rhode Island median, your plan lasts 3 years. If above, it lasts 5 years.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 allows you to catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan while keeping your home.

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate?

Chapter 13 dismissal rates vary. Nationally, about 33% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before completion. Working with an experienced attorney improves your chances of success.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Rhode Island Bankruptcy Exemptions -- What You Can Keep in Providence
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/exemptions.html

Providence Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you can keep when filing bankruptcy. Rhode Island has specific exemption amounts that protect your home, car, and personal property.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Key Rhode Island Exemptions

Property TypeExemption AmountDetails

Homestead$500,000Equity in your primary residence

Vehicle$12,000Equity in one motor vehicle

Wildcard$6,500Applied to any property

Household goodsVariesFurniture, appliances, clothing

Retirement accountsUnlimited401(k), IRA, pension (ERISA-qualified)

Social SecurityUnlimitedFully exempt under federal law

Tools of tradeVariesEquipment needed for your job

Rhode Island requires filers to use state exemptions. You cannot choose federal exemptions.

How Exemptions Work

Exemptions protect the equity in your property, not its total value. Equity is what your property is worth minus what you owe on it.

Example: If your car is worth $10,000 and you owe $7,000 on the loan, you have $3,000 in equity. If Rhode Island's vehicle exemption covers that equity, you keep the car.

In Chapter 7, non-exempt property may be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, you keep all property but must pay unsecured creditors at least the value of your non-exempt assets over your plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the homestead exemption in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your primary residence.

What is the vehicle exemption in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island's vehicle exemption protects up to $12,000 of equity in your car, truck, or motorcycle.

Can I choose federal exemptions in Rhode Island?

No. Rhode Island requires filers to use state exemptions.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Providence, Rhode Island?
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/cost.html

Providence Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Providence?

A complete breakdown of filing fees, attorney costs, and other expenses for bankruptcy in Providence, Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Fees

ChapterCourt Filing FeeTypical Attorney FeeTotal Estimated Cost

Chapter 7$338$1,200 to $2,200$1,538 - $2,538+

Chapter 13$313$3,500 to $5,000$3,813 - $5,313+

Attorney fees vary based on case complexity. Chapter 13 attorney fees are typically paid through your repayment plan.

Additional Costs

ExpenseCostNotes

Credit counseling (pre-filing)$10 - $25Required before filing

Debtor education (post-filing)$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free annually from each bureau

Document preparationVariesUsually included in attorney fee

Fee Waivers and Free Legal Aid

If your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you may qualify for a fee waiver (Chapter 7 only). You can also request to pay the filing fee in installments.

Free legal aid in Rhode Island:

Rhode Island Legal Services

Visit howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com for a detailed national cost guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Providence?

The filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,200 to $2,200. You also need two credit counseling courses ($25-$50 total).

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Providence?

The filing fee is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $3,500 to $5,000. Most Chapter 13 attorney fees can be paid through your plan.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

If your income is below 150% of the poverty line, you can apply to have the filing fee waived. You may also qualify for free legal aid services.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Bankruptcy FAQ -- Common Questions for Providence, Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/faq.html

Providence Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Bankruptcy FAQ for Providence, Rhode Island

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Providence.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Providence?

You file a bankruptcy petition with the D.R.I.. Most people hire an attorney, though you can file pro se. Before filing, you must complete a credit counseling course.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 in Providence?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you repay debts over 3-5 years while keeping property. Your income, assets, and goals determine the best choice.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect immediately when you file and stops all garnishments, lawsuits, and collection calls.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people see their credit scores begin to recover within 1-2 years of filing.

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island's vehicle exemption protects up to $12,000 of equity. If your equity is within the exemption, you keep the car. In Chapter 13, you continue making payments through your plan.

Can I keep my house if I file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity. In Chapter 13, you can catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan.

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical debt is unsecured debt that is dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy.

Do I have to go to court?

You must attend the 341 meeting of creditors, which is usually held at a meeting room (not a courtroom). Your attorney handles most court appearances.

Can I file bankruptcy if I am married?

Yes. You can file individually or jointly with your spouse. Filing jointly saves on attorney fees and filing costs.

Can I file bankruptcy again?

After a Chapter 7 discharge, wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After a Chapter 13 discharge, wait 2 years for another Chapter 13 or 6 years for Chapter 7. Use the 1328(f) screener at 1328f.com to check eligibility.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Providence Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/means-test.html

Providence Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Providence Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines whether you can file Chapter 7 bankruptcy. Your income is compared to the Rhode Island median for your household size.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

2026 Rhode Island Median Income Thresholds

Household SizeAnnual Median Income

1 person$63,147

2 persons$80,655

3 persons$97,449

4 persons$119,467

Each additional personAdd approximately $9,900

If your annualized income is below the median for your household size, you pass the means test automatically and qualify for Chapter 7.

Step-by-Step Means Test Walkthrough

1Calculate your current monthly income (CMI)

Add up all income from all sources for the six full calendar months before filing. Divide by six for monthly average. Multiply by 12 for annualized figure.

2Determine your household size

Count everyone who lives in your household and depends on your income -- yourself, spouse, and dependent children.

3Compare to Rhode Island median income

If your annualized CMI is at or below the median, you pass. No further calculation needed.

4If above median: Calculate disposable income

Subtract IRS-approved living expenses, secured debt payments, and priority debts. If remaining disposable income (x60) is less than $9,075, you pass.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the median income for the means test in Rhode Island in 2026?

The 2026 Rhode Island median income thresholds are: 1-person household $63,147, 2-person $80,655, 3-person $97,449, 4-person $119,467. Add approximately $9,900 for each additional household member.

What happens if I fail the means test?

You can file Chapter 13 instead (no means test), challenge with special circumstances, wait until your income drops, or re-examine deductions with an attorney.

Does the means test apply to Chapter 13?

No, but your income level determines whether your Chapter 13 plan lasts 3 years (below median) or 5 years (above median).

What income counts for the means test?

Average monthly income from all sources over the six full calendar months before filing. Includes wages, business income, rental income, pensions. Social Security is excluded from the initial median comparison.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## 341 Meeting of Creditors in Providence, Rhode Island -- What to Expect
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/341-meeting.html

Providence Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

341 Meeting of Creditors in Providence

The 341 meeting is a brief, required hearing in every bankruptcy case. Here is what Providence residents need to know.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Happens at the 341 Meeting

About 30 days after filing, you attend a meeting of creditors (named after Section 341 of the Bankruptcy Code). Despite the name, creditors rarely attend. The bankruptcy trustee runs the meeting.

1Identity verification

Bring a government-issued photo ID and proof of Social Security number (card, tax return, or W-2).

2Oath

You are placed under oath. All answers must be truthful.

3Trustee questions

The trustee asks about your petition, assets, debts, income, and recent transactions. Most questions confirm what is already in your paperwork.

4Creditor questions (rare)

Creditors may attend and ask questions, but this is uncommon in consumer cases.

Meeting Location

District: D.R.I.

Courthouse: 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903

Some 341 meetings may be held by telephone or video conference. Your attorney will confirm the format and location.

What to Bring

Government-issued photo ID (driver's license, passport, or state ID)

Proof of Social Security number (card, W-2, or tax return)

Recent pay stubs (last 60 days)

Most recent tax return

Bank statements (last 3-6 months)

Vehicle titles or registration

Any documents your attorney requests

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a 341 meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors is a brief hearing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and debts. It is required in every bankruptcy case.

Where is the 341 meeting held in Providence?

341 meetings for the D.R.I. are typically held at or near the courthouse at 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903. Some meetings may be conducted by phone or video.

What questions are asked at the 341 meeting?

The trustee will verify your identity, confirm the accuracy of your petition, ask about your assets and debts, and inquire about recent financial transactions.

How long does the 341 meeting last?

Most 341 meetings last 5-10 minutes. If your case is straightforward and your paperwork is complete, it should be brief.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Data sourced from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008--2024) and the U.S. Trustee Program.

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

## Bankruptcy Guide for Warwick, Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/providence/suburbs/warwick.html

Bankruptcy Guide for Warwick, Rhode Island

Bankruptcy information for residents of Warwick (Kent County). Warwick filers use Rhode Island state exemptions and file in the D.R.I..

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

$500,000

Homestead Exemption

$12,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Filing Bankruptcy in Warwick

Residents of Warwick (Kent County, population approximately 82,000) file bankruptcy in the D.R.I.. The same Rhode Island exemptions apply to all filers in the state.

Key Rhode Island exemptions:

Homestead: $500,000 of equity in your home

Vehicle: $12,000 of equity in your car

Retirement: Unlimited for ERISA-qualified plans

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13

Chapter 7

Eliminate most unsecured debt

Takes 3-4 months

Must pass means test

Filing fee: $338

Chapter 13

Repay debts over 3-5 years

Keep all property

No means test

Filing fee: $313

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Warwick residents file for bankruptcy?

Residents of Warwick, Rhode Island file in the D.R.I.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Warwick?

Filing fees are the same across Rhode Island: $338 for Chapter 7 and $313 for Chapter 13. Attorney fees in the Providence area typically range from $1,200 to $2,200 for Chapter 7.

What exemptions apply in Warwick?

Warwick residents use Rhode Island state exemptions: homestead $500,000, vehicle $12,000.

## The Automatic Stay in Rhode Island Bankruptcy
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/automatic-stay.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

The Automatic Stay in Rhode Island Bankruptcy

The moment you file bankruptcy in Rhode Island, a powerful federal court order called the automatic stay goes into effect. It immediately stops most creditor actions against you. Here is what it covers and what it does not.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What the Automatic Stay Stops Immediately

Stopped by the Stay

Wage garnishment -- Your employer must stop withholding garnished wages once notified of the filing

Foreclosure -- All foreclosure proceedings and sales are halted, even if a sale date is already scheduled

Vehicle repossession -- Creditors cannot repossess your car, truck, or other vehicles

Lawsuits -- All pending civil lawsuits for debt collection are paused

Creditor calls and letters -- All collection contacts must stop

Bank account levies -- Creditors cannot freeze or seize your bank account

Utility shutoffs -- Utilities cannot be disconnected for 20 days after filing (you must provide adequate assurance of future payment)

IRS collection -- Tax liens and levies are paused (though the lien itself survives)

Eviction -- In some circumstances, pending eviction actions are paused

NOT Stopped by the Stay

Child support collection -- Domestic support obligations (child support, alimony) continue

Criminal proceedings -- Criminal cases, including those involving bad checks or fraud, continue

Tax audits -- The IRS can continue auditing your tax returns (but cannot collect)

Pension loan repayment -- Deductions from wages for pension plan loan repayments continue

Paternity and custody -- Family court proceedings regarding custody, visitation, and paternity continue

Domestic violence proceedings -- Protective orders and related actions continue

Government regulatory actions -- Actions to enforce police or regulatory powers

Post-judgment evictions -- If the landlord already has a judgment for possession before filing

Stay Violations -- What to Do in Rhode Island

If a creditor continues collection activity after you file bankruptcy in Rhode Island, that is a violation of the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k). Common violations include:

Continued phone calls or letters demanding payment

Repossessing a vehicle after the case was filed

Proceeding with a foreclosure sale

Continuing to garnish wages

Filing or continuing a lawsuit

Refusing to return property already seized

Steps to Take If Your Stay Is Violated

Document everything. Save letters, record dates and times of phone calls, take screenshots, keep voicemails.

Notify the creditor in writing. Send a letter informing them of your bankruptcy case number and filing date.

Notify your attorney. Your attorney can file a motion for contempt and sanctions with the D.R.I. bankruptcy court.

You may be entitled to damages. Under section 362(k), individuals harmed by willful stay violations can recover actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

How Long Does the Automatic Stay Last?

Chapter 7

The stay lasts until your case is closed, dismissed, or your discharge is granted -- typically 3-4 months. After discharge, the discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524) permanently replaces the stay for discharged debts.

Chapter 13

The stay lasts for the duration of your 3-5 year repayment plan. This extended protection is one of Chapter 13's key advantages, particularly for saving homes and vehicles.

Repeat Filers: Reduced Stay Protection

If you had a bankruptcy case dismissed within the past year, special rules apply:

One prior dismissal: The automatic stay expires after 30 days unless you file a motion to extend it (section 362(c)(3)).

Two or more prior dismissals: The automatic stay does not go into effect at all unless the court imposes it (section 362(c)(4)).

Use the free 1328(f) Screener to check your eligibility before filing again.

Rhode Island-Specific Considerations

Rhode Island may be the smallest state, but its bankruptcy protections are substantial. The $500,000 homestead exemption and $12,000 vehicle exemption are among the most generous in the country. Judicial-only foreclosure adds another layer of debtor protection.

Foreclosure process: Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure. Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure, which requires the lender to go through the court system. This process is slower, giving homeowners more time to explore options like bankruptcy or loan modification.

Garnishment rules: Rhode Island follows the federal standard for wage garnishment: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount exceeding 30 times the federal minimum wage per week.

Homestead exemption: $500,000 -- the stay protects your home while the exemption determines what you keep

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the automatic stay in bankruptcy?

The automatic stay is a federal court order under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect the instant you file bankruptcy. It immediately stops most collection actions including wage garnishment, foreclosure proceedings, repossession attempts, lawsuits, harassing phone calls, and utility shutoffs. It applies to all creditors in Rhode Island whether they know about the filing or not.

What does the automatic stay stop?

The automatic stay stops: wage garnishment, foreclosure sales and proceedings, vehicle repossession, debt collection lawsuits, creditor phone calls and letters, bank account levies, utility disconnection (for 20 days), eviction (in some cases), and IRS collection activity on tax debts.

What does the automatic stay NOT stop?

The automatic stay does not stop: child support and alimony collection (domestic support obligations), most criminal proceedings, certain tax audit proceedings, pension plan loan repayments, and evictions where the landlord already had a judgment for possession before filing.

What happens if a creditor violates the automatic stay?

If a creditor violates the automatic stay in Rhode Island, you may be entitled to actual damages, attorney fees, and in some cases punitive damages under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k). Document the violation and notify your attorney immediately. Your attorney can file a motion for contempt with the D.R.I. bankruptcy court.

Does the automatic stay apply if I filed bankruptcy before?

If you had a case dismissed within the past year, the stay in your new case lasts only 30 days unless extended by court order. If you had two or more dismissals in the past year, the stay does not go into effect at all without a court order. Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) screener at 1328f.com.

How long does the automatic stay last?

In Chapter 7, the stay lasts until your case is closed, dismissed, or discharge is granted (typically 3-4 months). In Chapter 13, it lasts for the duration of your 3-5 year repayment plan. The discharge injunction under section 524 permanently replaces the stay for discharged debts.

Learn More About the Automatic Stay

Visit our dedicated automatic stay resource for a comprehensive guide to how the stay works nationwide.

Automatic Stay Guide
Free Discharge Screener

## Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/credit-after-bankruptcy.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Bankruptcy is not the end of your financial life -- it is a fresh start. Here is a practical guide to rebuilding your credit after bankruptcy in Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Credit Score Timeline

Your credit score will drop significantly when you file bankruptcy, but the trajectory improves from there. Here is what to expect:

Filing day: Your score drops, but if you were already behind on payments, the drop may be smaller than expected

Discharge (3-4 months for Ch. 7): The baseline from which rebuilding begins

6-12 months post-discharge: Score begins climbing with responsible credit use

12-24 months: Many filers reach 650+ with disciplined rebuilding

2-3 years: Scores of 700+ are achievable

4+ years: FHA and conventional mortgage options become available

Step-by-Step Rebuilding Strategy

1

Check Your Reports

Get free reports from AnnualCreditReport.com. Verify all discharged debts show $0 balance. Dispute any errors.

2

Secured Credit Card

Open a secured card with a $200-$500 deposit. Use it for one small recurring purchase. Pay in full monthly.

3

Credit Builder Loan

Many credit unions in Rhode Island offer credit builder loans. The money goes into savings while you make payments, building payment history.

4

Authorized User

Ask a trusted family member to add you as an authorized user on their card. Their positive payment history helps your score.

5

Unsecured Card

After 6-12 months of on-time payments, apply for an unsecured card. Keep utilization below 30%.

6

Diversify Credit

Over time, add different types of credit (installment loan, auto loan). Payment diversity improves your score mix.

Buying a Home After Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

After Chapter 7

FHA loan: 2 years after discharge

VA loan: 2 years after discharge

USDA loan: 3 years after discharge

Conventional: 4 years after discharge

Rhode Island's homestead exemption of $500,000 will protect your new home equity in any future financial difficulty.

After Chapter 13

FHA loan: 1 year into plan (with court permission) or at discharge

VA loan: 1 year into plan or at discharge

USDA loan: 1 year after discharge

Conventional: 2 years after discharge

Chapter 13's shorter waiting periods are an advantage for those planning to buy a home.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Applying for too much credit at once. Each application creates a hard inquiry that temporarily lowers your score. Space applications 3-6 months apart.

Carrying balances. Pay your credit card balance in full every month. Carrying a balance does not build credit faster -- it just costs you interest.

Ignoring your credit reports. Check your reports regularly. Discharged debts sometimes still appear as owing a balance, which drags down your score.

Payday loans and predatory lenders. Avoid high-interest lenders that target people after bankruptcy. They trap you in a cycle of debt.

Co-signing loans. Do not co-sign for anyone until your own credit is fully rebuilt. If they default, you are responsible.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report in Rhode Island?

Chapter 7 bankruptcy remains on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 remains for 7 years from the filing date. However, your credit score can begin improving much sooner than that.

Can I get a credit card after bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. Many people receive credit card offers shortly after discharge. Secured credit cards (where you provide a deposit) are the most common starting point. Use the card for small purchases and pay the balance in full each month to rebuild your credit score.

How quickly can my credit score improve after bankruptcy?

Many people see meaningful credit score improvement within 12-18 months after discharge. Some filers report scores above 700 within 2-3 years by following disciplined rebuilding strategies.

Can I buy a house after bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. FHA loans are available 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge and 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan (with court permission). Conventional loans typically require a 4-year waiting period after Chapter 7 and 2 years after Chapter 13 discharge.

Can I buy a car after bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. Auto loans are available almost immediately after discharge, though interest rates will be higher initially. Rates improve as your credit score recovers. Putting a larger down payment can help secure better terms.

Check Your Discharge Eligibility

Use our free screener to check if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge before taking steps to rebuild.

Free Discharge Screener
Credit Rebuilding Guide

## Keep Your Car in Rhode Island Bankruptcy
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/keep-your-car.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Keep Your Car in Rhode Island Bankruptcy

One of the biggest fears people have about bankruptcy is losing their car. The good news: most people in Rhode Island keep their vehicles. Here is how car protection works in Rhode Island bankruptcy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Rhode Island's Vehicle Exemption: $12,000

Rhode Island's $12,000 vehicle exemption is among the most generous in the nation. Most filers can protect their car entirely under this exemption.

How to calculate your equity: Take the fair market value of your car (check KBB or NADA) and subtract what you still owe on the loan. The result is your equity.

Example 1: Car worth $10,000, you owe $8,000 = $2,000 equity. Protected under the $12,000 exemption.

Example 2: Car worth $15,000, you owe $5,000 = $10,000 equity. Exceeds the exemption -- but options exist.

Example 3: Car worth $6,000, you owe $9,000 = negative equity. Fully protected (no equity for trustee to take).

Your Options for Keeping Your Car

Chapter 7 Options

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car and loan survive your bankruptcy.

Redemption: Pay the car's current market value in one lump sum, even if you owe more.

Surrender: Give back the car and discharge the remaining debt if the car is not worth keeping.

Chapter 13 Options

Catch up on arrears: Spread missed payments over your 3-5 year plan while keeping the car.

Cramdown: If the loan is more than 910 days old, reduce the balance to the car's actual value.

Reduce interest rate: The court may lower your interest rate to a market rate.

The 910-Day Rule (Cramdown)

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a), if you purchased your car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing Chapter 13, you can "cram down" the loan to the car's current value.

Example: You bought a car 3 years ago for $25,000. You still owe $18,000, but the car is now worth $12,000. Through a cramdown, you would only pay $12,000 through your plan (plus interest), and the remaining $6,000 is treated as unsecured debt.

If you purchased the car within the last 910 days, you must pay the full loan balance through your plan.

What About Repossession?

If you are behind on payments and facing repossession, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately stops repossession efforts.

Before repo: Filing stops the repossession from happening

After repo but before sale: You may be able to get the car back through bankruptcy

After the car is sold: Recovery is much more difficult, but you may be able to discharge the deficiency balance

If repossession is imminent, timing is critical. Contact an attorney or legal aid immediately.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

In most cases, yes. Rhode Island's vehicle exemption protects up to $12,000 of equity in your car. If your equity is below that amount, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car while catching up on payments through your repayment plan.

What is Rhode Island's vehicle exemption?

Rhode Island allows you to exempt up to $12,000 of equity in a motor vehicle. Equity means the vehicle's market value minus what you owe on it. Rhode Island's $12,000 vehicle exemption is among the most generous in the nation. Most filers can protect their car entirely under this exemption.

What if my car equity exceeds the exemption?

If your car equity exceeds Rhode Island's $12,000 exemption, you have options: negotiate to pay the trustee the non-exempt amount, convert to Chapter 13 (where you keep property and pay through a plan), or use other available exemptions to cover the difference.

What is reaffirmation?

Reaffirmation is an agreement to continue paying a car loan after Chapter 7 bankruptcy. You keep the car and the loan, and the debt survives your discharge. This is common when you are current on payments and want to keep the car.

What is vehicle redemption in bankruptcy?

Redemption allows you to pay the current market value of the car in one lump sum, regardless of what you owe. If you owe $15,000 but the car is worth $8,000, you can pay $8,000 to keep it. This requires full payment at once (or through a redemption lender).

Can Chapter 13 help me keep my car?

Yes. Chapter 13 lets you catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years while keeping your vehicle. If the loan is more than 910 days old (about 2.5 years), you may be able to reduce the loan balance to the car's current value through a cramdown.

Check Your Discharge Eligibility

Use our free screener to see if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge that could eliminate your car debt.

Free Discharge Screener
Keep Your Car Guide

Browse Other States

Iowa
Maryland
Minnesota
Rhode Island

## Keep Your House in Rhode Island Bankruptcy
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/keep-your-house.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Keep Your House in Rhode Island Bankruptcy

Your home is often your most important asset. Here is how Rhode Island's $500,000 homestead exemption and bankruptcy law work together to help you keep your house.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Rhode Island's Homestead Exemption: $500,000

Rhode Island offers a strong homestead exemption of up to $500,000 for your primary residence. This is one of the highest homestead exemptions in the country and protects most Rhode Island homeowners filing bankruptcy.

How to calculate your home equity: Take the fair market value of your home and subtract all mortgages and liens. The result is your equity.

Example 1: Home worth $250,000, mortgage balance $200,000 = $50,000 equity. Protected under Rhode Island's $500,000 exemption.

Example 2: Home worth $400,000, mortgage balance $150,000 = $250,000 equity. Check if this exceeds Rhode Island's exemption limit.

Example 3: Home worth $180,000, mortgage balance $200,000 = negative equity. Fully protected (no equity for trustee to take).

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Your Home

Chapter 7

Homestead exemption protects equity up to $500,000

Eliminates unsecured debt, making mortgage payments more affordable

Does NOT stop foreclosure long-term -- only delays it during the case

You must stay current on mortgage payments to keep the home

Best if you are current on mortgage and need to eliminate other debts

Chapter 13

Stops foreclosure through the automatic stay

Catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years

May strip off underwater second mortgages

Protects home regardless of equity amount

Best if you are behind on mortgage payments

Foreclosure in Rhode Island

Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure, which requires the lender to go through the court system. This process is slower, giving homeowners more time to explore options like bankruptcy or loan modification.

Timeline: Judicial foreclosure in Rhode Island typically takes 6-12 months, providing substantial time for homeowners to explore alternatives.

If you are facing foreclosure in Rhode Island, bankruptcy can stop the process. The automatic stay halts all foreclosure proceedings the moment you file. Chapter 13 then gives you a structured way to catch up on missed payments while keeping your home.

Lien Stripping in Chapter 13

If your home is "underwater" on the first mortgage (you owe more than the home is worth), Chapter 13 allows you to strip off junior liens like second mortgages and HELOCs. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt and may be discharged at the end of your plan.

Example: Your home is worth $200,000. First mortgage: $210,000. Second mortgage: $50,000. Because the first mortgage exceeds the home's value, the $50,000 second mortgage can be stripped and treated as unsecured debt, potentially saving you $50,000.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my house if I file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

In most cases, yes. Rhode Island's homestead exemption protects up to $500,000 of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, your home is fully protected in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments through a 3-5 year repayment plan.

What is Rhode Island's homestead exemption?

Rhode Island offers a strong homestead exemption of up to $500,000 for your primary residence. This is one of the highest homestead exemptions in the country and protects most Rhode Island homeowners filing bankruptcy.

Can Chapter 13 stop foreclosure in Rhode Island?

Yes. Filing Chapter 13 triggers the automatic stay, which immediately stops foreclosure proceedings. You can then catch up on missed mortgage payments over 3-5 years through your repayment plan while continuing to make regular monthly payments going forward.

How does foreclosure work in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure. Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure, which requires the lender to go through the court system. This process is slower, giving homeowners more time to explore options like bankruptcy or loan modification.

What happens to my second mortgage in bankruptcy?

If your home is worth less than what you owe on the first mortgage, Chapter 13 may allow you to strip off (remove) the second mortgage entirely. The second mortgage is treated as unsecured debt. This is called lien stripping and can save homeowners thousands.

Protect Your Home Today

Learn about your options and check your discharge eligibility with our free tools.

## Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/medical-debt.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. If you are overwhelmed by medical bills in Rhode Island, bankruptcy can eliminate that debt and give you a fresh start.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Medical Debt Is Fully Dischargeable

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured debt under bankruptcy law. This means it receives no special protection and is typically eliminated entirely in bankruptcy.

Chapter 7: Medical debt is wiped out completely within 3-4 months of filing

Chapter 13: Medical debt is included in your repayment plan, and any unpaid balance is discharged at the end

No minimum amount: You can file bankruptcy whether you owe $5,000 or $500,000 in medical debt

All providers included: Hospital bills, doctor bills, ambulance fees, lab work, prescription drugs -- all dischargeable

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Medical Debt

Chapter 7

Eliminates 100% of medical debt

Completed in 3-4 months

Must pass the means test

Non-exempt assets may be sold by trustee

Rhode Island exemptions: $500,000 homestead, $12,000 vehicle

Best for: people with primarily unsecured debt and limited assets

Chapter 13

Pay what you can afford over 3-5 years

Remaining medical debt discharged at end

No means test requirement

Keep all your property

Can also catch up on mortgage/car payments

Best for: people with assets to protect or higher income

Before Filing: Alternatives to Consider

Negotiate directly. Many hospitals offer significant discounts for uninsured patients or will reduce bills by 30-60% for prompt payment.

Financial assistance programs. Non-profit hospitals are required to have charity care policies. Ask about financial hardship applications.

Payment plans. Most providers will set up interest-free payment plans. Make sure any plan is affordable -- do not agree to payments you cannot sustain.

Statute of limitations. Medical debt has a limited collection window. If the debt is old, check whether it is still legally enforceable in Rhode Island.

Medical billing errors. Studies show a significant percentage of medical bills contain errors. Request itemized bills and dispute anything incorrect.

If these alternatives do not resolve your situation, bankruptcy remains a powerful tool for eliminating medical debt entirely.

Rhode Island-Specific Medical Debt Rules

Rhode Island follows the federal standard for wage garnishment: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount exceeding 30 times the federal minimum wage per week.

Medical providers who obtain a judgment against you in Rhode Island can use wage garnishment, bank levies, and property liens to collect. Filing bankruptcy stops all of these collection methods through the automatic stay.

Legal aid: Rhode Island Legal Services (rils.org) may offer free assistance with medical debt and bankruptcy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy eliminate medical debt?

Yes. Medical debt is unsecured debt, which is typically fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, medical debt is eliminated entirely within 3-4 months. In Chapter 13, you may pay a portion through your plan, with the remainder discharged.

Can a hospital sue me for medical debt in Rhode Island?

Yes. Hospitals and medical providers can sue to collect unpaid medical bills in Rhode Island. If they win a judgment, they may be able to garnish your wages or levy your bank accounts. Filing bankruptcy stops all lawsuits and collection actions through the automatic stay.

Will bankruptcy stop medical debt wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay immediately stops all wage garnishment, including garnishment for medical debt. In Rhode Island, Rhode Island follows the federal standard for wage garnishment: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount exceeding 30 times the federal minimum wage per week.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 for medical debt?

Chapter 7 is usually preferred for medical debt because it eliminates the debt completely in 3-4 months with no repayment. Chapter 13 is better if you have assets to protect that exceed Rhode Island's exemptions, or if you do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test.

Does medical debt affect my credit score?

Yes, but less than before. Since 2023, paid medical debt no longer appears on credit reports. Unpaid medical debt under $500 also no longer appears. Medical debt in collections still affects your score but is given less weight than other types of debt by newer scoring models.

Overwhelmed by Medical Bills?

Check your eligibility for a bankruptcy discharge that could eliminate your medical debt.
