## Wage Garnishment and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/wage-garnishment.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Wage Garnishment and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

If your paycheck is being garnished, filing bankruptcy can stop it immediately. Here is how wage garnishment works in Rhode Island and how bankruptcy provides relief.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Rhode Island Wage Garnishment Rules

Rhode Island follows the federal standard for wage garnishment: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount exceeding 30 times the federal minimum wage per week.

How garnishment works: A creditor must first sue you and win a judgment. Then they file a garnishment order with the court, which is served on your employer. Your employer is legally required to withhold the garnished amount from your paycheck.

Exceptions that do not require a judgment:

Federal student loan garnishment (up to 15% of disposable pay)

Child support and alimony

Federal and state tax debts

How Bankruptcy Stops Garnishment

Immediately Stopped

Credit card debt garnishment

Medical bill garnishment

Personal loan garnishment

Auto deficiency garnishment

Student loan garnishment

Most judgment garnishments

NOT Stopped

Child support withholding

Alimony withholding

Domestic support obligations

The automatic stay takes effect the instant you file your bankruptcy petition. Your attorney will notify your employer and the garnishing creditor. Garnishment must stop immediately -- any wages garnished after filing may need to be returned to you.

Recovering Previously Garnished Wages

Under 11 U.S.C. section 547, the bankruptcy trustee may be able to recover wages garnished within 90 days before your filing date if the total exceeds $600. These recovered funds become part of your bankruptcy estate and may be distributed to creditors or exempted by you.

This is another reason why timing your bankruptcy filing strategically matters. Consulting with an attorney or legal aid about the best time to file can maximize your recovery.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Garnishment

Chapter 7

Stops garnishment immediately

Discharges the underlying debt in 3-4 months

Garnishment cannot resume for discharged debts

Must pass the means test

Chapter 13

Stops garnishment immediately

Repay debts through a structured plan instead

Better for debts that cannot be discharged in Chapter 7

3-5 year repayment period

Frequently Asked Questions

How much of my wages can be garnished in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island follows the federal standard for wage garnishment: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount exceeding 30 times the federal minimum wage per week.

Can bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy in Rhode Island triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362, which immediately stops all wage garnishment. Your employer will be notified and must stop withholding garnished wages.

Can I get back wages that were already garnished?

In some cases, yes. If wages were garnished within 90 days before filing and exceed $600, the bankruptcy trustee may be able to recover those funds as a preferential transfer. Discuss this with your attorney.

What debts can lead to wage garnishment?

In general, any creditor who obtains a court judgment can garnish wages. Common sources include credit card debt, medical bills, personal loans, and auto deficiency balances. Child support and tax debts can be garnished without a court judgment.

Does the automatic stay stop all types of garnishment in Rhode Island?

The automatic stay stops garnishment for most debts, but not for domestic support obligations (child support and alimony). Tax garnishment is also stopped temporarily. Student loan garnishment by the government is stopped during the bankruptcy case.

Stop Garnishment Now

Check your eligibility for bankruptcy and learn how to stop wage garnishment.

## Student Loans and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/student-loans.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Student Loans and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Student loan debt is a growing crisis. While discharging student loans in bankruptcy has historically been difficult, recent changes are making it more achievable for Rhode Island borrowers.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Changing Landscape of Student Loan Discharge

For decades, the conventional wisdom was that student loans could never be discharged in bankruptcy. That was never entirely true, and recent developments have shifted the landscape significantly:

November 2022: DOJ issued new guidance creating a more structured, less adversarial process for evaluating student loan discharge

Brunner reconsideration: Several circuit courts have reconsidered the rigid application of the Brunner test

Adversary proceedings: Filing an adversary proceeding (a lawsuit within your bankruptcy case) is required, but the process is becoming more straightforward

Partial discharge: Even if full discharge is not granted, courts can discharge a portion of student loan debt

The Undue Hardship Standard

Brunner Test (3 Prongs)

Cannot maintain minimal standard of living if forced to repay the loan

Hardship likely to persist for a significant portion of the repayment period

Good faith effort to repay (applied for deferments, IDR plans, made payments when able)

Totality of Circumstances

Some courts use a more flexible test that considers all relevant factors:

Past, present, and future financial resources

Reasonable living expenses

Other relevant facts and circumstances

No rigid three-prong requirement

Alternatives to Bankruptcy for Student Loans

Income-Driven Repayment (IDR): Plans like SAVE, IBR, PAYE, and ICR cap payments at 5-20% of discretionary income. Remaining balance forgiven after 20-25 years.

Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF): Full forgiveness after 120 qualifying payments while working for government or non-profit employers.

Total and Permanent Disability (TPD): Full discharge if you have a qualifying disability.

Closed School Discharge: If your school closed while you were enrolled or shortly after.

Borrower Defense: If your school engaged in fraud or misrepresentation.

If none of these alternatives resolve your student loan burden, bankruptcy with an adversary proceeding for undue hardship is a viable path.

Filing in Rhode Island

Student loan adversary proceedings in Rhode Island are filed in the District of Rhode Island. The process involves:

File your Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 bankruptcy case

File a separate adversary proceeding (complaint) seeking discharge of student loans

The DOJ or loan servicer responds

Discovery, mediation, or trial may follow

The court rules on whether undue hardship is established

Courthouse: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903

Legal assistance: Rhode Island Legal Services (rils.org) may provide guidance or representation for student loan adversary proceedings.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can student loans be discharged in bankruptcy?

Yes, but it is harder than other debts. Student loans can be discharged in bankruptcy by filing an adversary proceeding and proving undue hardship. Recent DOJ guidance (November 2022) has made this process more accessible, and some courts in the District of Rhode Island may apply a more flexible standard.

What is the Brunner test for student loans?

The Brunner test requires you to show: (1) you cannot maintain a minimal standard of living if forced to repay, (2) your financial situation is likely to persist for a significant portion of the repayment period, and (3) you have made good faith efforts to repay. Some courts use the totality of circumstances test instead, which is generally more flexible.

What did the DOJ change about student loan discharge?

In November 2022, the Department of Justice issued new guidance making it easier to discharge student loans in bankruptcy. The DOJ now considers factors like income, expenses, loan balance, and repayment history in a more holistic way, rather than applying the Brunner test rigidly.

What about income-driven repayment plans?

Income-driven repayment (IDR) plans like SAVE, IBR, PAYE, and ICR can reduce monthly payments to as low as $0 based on income. However, they do not eliminate the debt. Bankruptcy may be a better option if the total debt is unmanageable even with reduced payments.

Does filing bankruptcy stop student loan collection?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection activity, including wage garnishment for federal student loans, lawsuits, and collection calls. This protection lasts for the duration of your bankruptcy case.

Explore Your Options

Check your discharge eligibility and learn about your rights as a borrower.

## Tax Debt and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/tax-debt.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Tax Debt and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Tax debt can feel insurmountable, but bankruptcy may offer relief. Some tax debts can be discharged in bankruptcy if they meet specific requirements.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Tax Discharge Tests

To discharge federal income tax debt in bankruptcy, ALL of the following must be true:

3-Year Rule: The tax return was due (including extensions) more than 3 years before you filed bankruptcy

2-Year Rule: The tax return was actually filed more than 2 years before filing bankruptcy

240-Day Rule: The tax was assessed by the IRS more than 240 days before filing bankruptcy

No fraud: The return was not fraudulent

No willful evasion: You did not willfully attempt to evade the tax

Example: Your 2021 tax return was due April 15, 2022. If you file bankruptcy after April 15, 2025, the 3-year test is met. If you actually filed the return before April 2023, the 2-year test is also met. If the IRS assessed the tax before July 2024, the 240-day test is met.

What Taxes Cannot Be Discharged

Recent taxes: Tax debts that do not meet the 3-year/2-year/240-day tests

Payroll taxes: Trust fund taxes (employee withholding) are never dischargeable

Fraudulent returns: Taxes on returns filed with intent to defraud

Unfiled returns: Taxes for years where no return was filed (or filed late and less than 2 years ago)

Tax penalties: Some penalties may survive, depending on their nature and age

State taxes: Rhode Island state tax rules may differ from federal rules -- consult a tax professional

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Tax Debt

Chapter 7

Can discharge qualifying tax debts entirely

Completed in 3-4 months

Non-qualifying tax debt survives the case

Tax liens survive even if the debt is discharged

Best if: your tax debt meets all discharge requirements

Chapter 13

Pay priority tax debts over 3-5 years

Stops penalties and interest during the plan

Automatic stay stops all IRS collection

Non-priority tax debts may be partially discharged

Best if: tax debt does not qualify for Chapter 7 discharge

Tax Liens and Bankruptcy

A critical distinction: even when the underlying tax debt is discharged, a recorded IRS tax lien survives bankruptcy. The lien remains attached to property you owned at the time of filing.

However, the personal obligation to pay is eliminated. This means the IRS cannot garnish your wages or levy your bank accounts, but the lien remains on existing property until it expires or is satisfied.

Strategy: If you have no significant assets, a surviving lien has little practical effect. If you do have property, Chapter 13 may be more effective because it allows you to pay the tax through your plan and clear the lien.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can you discharge tax debt in bankruptcy?

Some tax debts can be discharged in bankruptcy, but it depends on the type of tax, when it was due, and whether returns were filed. Income taxes that meet specific age and filing requirements may be dischargeable.

What taxes can be discharged in bankruptcy?

Only income taxes may be dischargeable, and they must meet all of these tests: (1) the tax return was due more than 3 years ago, (2) the return was filed more than 2 years ago, (3) the tax was assessed more than 240 days ago, and (4) the return was not fraudulent and you did not willfully evade the tax.

What about tax liens?

Even if the underlying tax debt is discharged, a recorded tax lien survives bankruptcy and remains attached to your property. The lien stays until it expires or you pay it. However, you can negotiate with the IRS to remove the lien after discharge in some cases.

Can bankruptcy stop an IRS levy or garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay immediately stops IRS levies, wage garnishment, and seizure of bank accounts or property. The IRS can continue auditing, but it cannot collect while the stay is in effect.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 for tax debt?

If your tax debt meets the discharge requirements, Chapter 7 can eliminate it entirely. If it does not qualify for discharge, Chapter 13 lets you pay it over 3-5 years without penalties and interest accumulating, and stops IRS collection actions during the plan.

Overwhelmed by Tax Debt?

Check your eligibility and explore your options for tax debt relief through bankruptcy.

## Small Business Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/small-business.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Small Business Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Running a small business in Rhode Island is hard enough without crushing debt. If your business is struggling, bankruptcy offers several paths -- from reorganization to an orderly wind-down.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Options for Rhode Island Business Owners

Chapter 7 Liquidation

Business assets are sold to pay creditors

Remaining debts are discharged (for individuals)

Business ceases to exist

Personal guarantees can be discharged

Fastest option: 3-4 months

Best for: businesses with no viable future

Chapter 11 Subchapter V

Continue operating the business

Restructure debts over 3-5 years

No creditor committee (usually)

Debtor remains in control

Debt limit: $7.5 million

Best for: viable businesses needing debt relief

Chapter 13 (Sole Proprietors)

Available only to individuals (sole proprietors)

Continue operating the business

Restructure personal and business debts

Debt limits apply (currently ~$2.75M)

3-5 year repayment plan

Best for: sole proprietors under the debt limits

Close + Personal Chapter 7

Shut down the business first

File personal Chapter 7 to discharge guarantees

Eliminates personal liability for business debts

Must pass the means test

Clean break: start fresh

Best for: sole proprietors with personal guarantees

Personal Guarantees: The Hidden Trap

Even if your business is an LLC or corporation, you may be personally liable for business debts if you signed personal guarantees. This is extremely common for:

Business leases (office, retail, equipment)

Business loans and lines of credit

Business credit cards

Vendor credit accounts

If your LLC or corporation cannot pay these debts, creditors will come after you personally. Filing personal bankruptcy can discharge these personal guarantee obligations.

Subchapter V: The Game-Changer for Small Business

The Small Business Reorganization Act of 2019 created Subchapter V of Chapter 11, dramatically simplifying the reorganization process for small businesses:

No creditors' committee: Saves significant legal costs

Trustee role: A Subchapter V trustee facilitates the case but does not take over operations

Faster timeline: Plan must be filed within 90 days

No absolute priority rule: Owners can retain equity without paying creditors in full

Consensual or nonconsensual plans: The court can confirm a plan even if some creditors object

Lower costs: Typically 50-75% less expensive than traditional Chapter 11

Cases are filed in the District of Rhode Island. Courthouse: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903

Frequently Asked Questions

What are my bankruptcy options as a small business owner in Rhode Island?

Small business owners in Rhode Island have several options: Chapter 7 (liquidation of business assets), Chapter 11 Subchapter V (streamlined reorganization for businesses with less than $7.5 million in debt), Chapter 13 (if you are a sole proprietor with debts under the limit), or simply closing the business and filing personal Chapter 7 to discharge personal guarantees.

What is Subchapter V of Chapter 11?

Subchapter V is a streamlined Chapter 11 process created by the Small Business Reorganization Act of 2019. It is designed for businesses with less than $7.5 million in non-contingent, liquidated debt. It is faster, cheaper, and more debtor-friendly than traditional Chapter 11.

Am I personally liable for business debts?

It depends on your business structure. Sole proprietors are personally liable for all business debts. LLC members and corporate shareholders are generally not personally liable unless they signed personal guarantees, pierced the corporate veil, or committed fraud. Personal guarantees are the most common source of personal liability.

Can I keep my business and file bankruptcy?

Yes. Chapter 11 Subchapter V and Chapter 13 (for sole proprietors) allow you to continue operating your business while reorganizing debts. The business remains under your control, and you propose a repayment plan.

What happens to my employees if I file business bankruptcy?

If you reorganize under Chapter 11 or Chapter 13, you can continue employing your staff. If you liquidate under Chapter 7, employees are typically let go. Unpaid wages are a priority claim in bankruptcy and are paid before general creditors.

Explore Your Business Options

Whether you want to reorganize or start fresh, learn about your paths forward.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Comparison -- chapter7vs13.org

Open Bankruptcy Project -- openbankruptcyproject.org

---

## Types of Debt in Rhode Island Bankruptcy
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/debt-types.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Types of Debt in Rhode Island Bankruptcy

Not all debts are treated equally in bankruptcy. Understanding which debts can be eliminated and which survive is essential for making the right filing decision in Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Dischargeable Debts (Can Be Eliminated)

Credit card debt: Fully dischargeable in Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Medical bills: Fully dischargeable -- the most common reason for filing

Personal loans: Including payday loans, signature loans, and promissory notes

Utility arrears: Past-due electric, gas, water, and phone bills

Old tax debt: Income taxes meeting the 3-year/2-year/240-day tests

Deficiency balances: Remaining debt after repossession or foreclosure

Lawsuit judgments: Most money judgments from civil lawsuits

Lease obligations: Remaining rent or equipment lease payments

HOA fees: Past-due homeowner association assessments (pre-filing)

Non-Dischargeable Debts (Usually Survive)

Student loans: Generally survive unless you prove undue hardship in an adversary proceeding

Child support and alimony: Domestic support obligations are never dischargeable

Recent taxes: Tax debts that do not meet the discharge requirements

Fraud-based debts: Debts obtained through fraud, false pretenses, or false financial statements

DUI/DWI injury debts: Debts for death or personal injury caused by intoxicated driving

Criminal fines and restitution: Government fines and criminal restitution orders

Willful and malicious injury: Debts from intentional harm to person or property

Unlisted debts: Debts you fail to include in your bankruptcy schedules

Secured vs. Unsecured Debt

Secured Debt

Backed by collateral. The creditor has a lien on specific property.

Mortgage (secured by your home)

Car loan (secured by your vehicle)

Furniture/appliance loans

Home equity loans (HELOCs)

In bankruptcy: You typically must surrender the collateral, reaffirm the debt, or redeem the property. In Chapter 13, you can catch up on arrears through your plan.

Unsecured Debt

No collateral. The creditor's only remedy is to sue you for a money judgment.

Credit cards

Medical bills

Personal loans

Collection accounts

In bankruptcy: Usually eliminated entirely in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you pay what you can afford and the rest is discharged.

Priority Debt

Priority debts receive special treatment and must be paid in full in a Chapter 13 plan:

Domestic support obligations: Child support and alimony arrears

Certain tax debts: Recent income taxes, trust fund taxes

Employee wages: Unpaid wages earned within 180 days before filing (up to a limit)

Administrative expenses: Costs of administering the bankruptcy case

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts can be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most unsecured debts can be discharged, including credit card debt, medical bills, personal loans, utility arrears, old tax debt (if it meets age requirements), and deficiency balances from repossession or foreclosure. Some debts like student loans, recent taxes, child support, and fraud-based debts generally cannot be discharged.

What is the difference between secured and unsecured debt?

Secured debt is backed by collateral (like a mortgage or car loan). If you do not pay, the creditor can take the collateral. Unsecured debt has no collateral (like credit cards or medical bills). In bankruptcy, unsecured debt is usually eliminated entirely, while secured debt may require you to return the collateral or keep paying.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Non-dischargeable debts include: most student loans (absent undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts, debts from fraud or willful injury, criminal fines and restitution, and debts not listed in your bankruptcy schedules.

What is priority debt?

Priority debts are given special treatment in bankruptcy. They must be paid in full in Chapter 13 plans. Examples include recent tax debts, child support arrears, alimony arrears, and certain employee wages. Priority debts generally cannot be discharged in Chapter 7.

Does Rhode Island treat any debts differently?

Bankruptcy law is federal, so debt treatment is largely the same everywhere. However, Rhode Island's exemptions determine what property you can protect: $500,000 homestead, $12,000 vehicle. State law also governs the statute of limitations on debt collection and garnishment rules.

Find Out Which Debts You Can Eliminate

Use our free screener to check your discharge eligibility.

## Foreclosure and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/foreclosure.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Foreclosure and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Facing foreclosure in Rhode Island? Bankruptcy can stop the process immediately and give you time to save your home. Here is how judicial foreclosure works in Rhode Island and how bankruptcy provides relief.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Foreclosure Works in Rhode Island

Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure, which requires the lender to go through the court system. This process is slower, giving homeowners more time to explore options like bankruptcy or loan modification.

Timeline: Judicial foreclosure in Rhode Island typically takes 6-12 months, providing substantial time for homeowners to explore alternatives.

How Bankruptcy Stops Foreclosure

The automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362 immediately halts all foreclosure activity the moment you file bankruptcy. This includes:

Foreclosure lawsuits

Scheduled foreclosure sales

Sheriff's sales

Trustee's sales

Deficiency judgment actions

The stay applies even if the lender does not know about your filing yet. Once the lender is notified, any foreclosure action taken after filing is void.

Chapter 13: The Foreclosure Defense Tool

How Chapter 13 Saves Your Home

Stops foreclosure through the automatic stay

Catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years

Continue making regular monthly payments going forward

May strip off underwater second mortgages

Eliminate other debts to free up cash for mortgage

Rhode Island Homestead Protection

Homestead exemption: $500,000

Protects your home equity in bankruptcy

Applies in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Rhode Island offers a strong homestead exemption of up to $500,000 for your primary residence. This is one of the highest homestead exemptions in the country and protects most Rhode Island homeowners filing bankruptcy.

Timing Is Critical

The sooner you act, the more options you have:

Early stage (first missed payments): Contact your lender about forbearance or loan modification before considering bankruptcy

Pre-foreclosure notice received: Consult with an attorney or legal aid about bankruptcy options

Foreclosure filed: Filing bankruptcy still stops the process, but you have less time

Sale date scheduled: Emergency bankruptcy filing can stop the sale, but must be filed before the sale occurs

After the sale: Options are very limited -- in some states, a redemption period may still apply

Legal assistance: Rhode Island Legal Services (rils.org) may provide free help with foreclosure defense and bankruptcy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop foreclosure in Rhode Island?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts all foreclosure proceedings in Rhode Island. Chapter 13 is especially powerful because it allows you to catch up on missed mortgage payments over 3-5 years while keeping your home.

How does foreclosure work in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure. Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure, which requires the lender to go through the court system. This process is slower, giving homeowners more time to explore options like bankruptcy or loan modification.

How quickly do I need to file to stop a foreclosure sale?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your bankruptcy petition is filed. Even if a foreclosure sale is scheduled for tomorrow, filing today stops it. However, filing as early as possible gives you more options. Judicial foreclosure in Rhode Island typically takes 6-12 months, providing substantial time for homeowners to explore alternatives.

Can I save my home in Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 can delay foreclosure temporarily through the automatic stay, but it does not provide a long-term mechanism to catch up on missed payments. Rhode Island's $500,000 homestead exemption protects your equity, but you must be current on mortgage payments to keep the home. Chapter 13 is usually better for saving a home from foreclosure.

What is the redemption period in Rhode Island?

Rhode Island uses judicial foreclosure, which requires the lender to go through the court system. This process is slower, giving homeowners more time to explore options like bankruptcy or loan modification.

Facing Foreclosure?

Time is critical. Check your options and learn how bankruptcy can help save your home.

Free Discharge Screener
Keep Your House Guide

## Vehicle Repossession and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/repossession.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Vehicle Repossession and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Is your car about to be repossessed? Bankruptcy can stop repossession immediately and may help you get your vehicle back. Here is how it works in Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Before Repossession: Filing Stops It

If you are behind on your car payments and facing repossession, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362. The stay immediately prohibits the lender from:

Sending a tow truck to take your car

Hiring a repossession agent

Demanding you surrender the vehicle

Contacting you about the delinquent payments

The stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed -- even before the creditor is notified. However, you should inform the creditor as soon as possible to prevent a violation.

After Repossession: Your Options

If your car has already been repossessed, your options depend on whether it has been sold:

Car Not Yet Sold

Filing bankruptcy and requesting turnover of the vehicle may force the creditor to return it

Chapter 13 allows you to catch up on payments while keeping the car

You must act quickly -- creditors typically sell repossessed vehicles within 10-14 days

Car Already Sold

You generally cannot get the car back after it is sold

The deficiency balance (what you still owe after the sale) is unsecured debt

This deficiency can be discharged in Chapter 7 or Chapter 13

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 After Repossession

Chapter 7

Discharges deficiency balance after repo

Cannot catch up on missed payments

May require surrendering the car if you still have it

Reaffirmation or redemption options available

$12,000 vehicle exemption protects equity

Chapter 13

Catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years

May recover vehicle after repo (if not yet sold)

Cramdown option if loan is over 910 days old

Keep the vehicle while repaying through plan

Better for keeping your car long-term

Rhode Island Vehicle Protection

Rhode Island's $12,000 vehicle exemption is among the most generous in the nation. Most filers can protect their car entirely under this exemption.

See our full guide on keeping your car in Rhode Island bankruptcy for more details on exemptions, reaffirmation, redemption, and cramdown options.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop repossession in Rhode Island?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately prohibits creditors from repossessing your vehicle. If repossession is imminent, an emergency bankruptcy filing can stop it.

Can I get my car back after repossession?

If the car has not been sold yet, filing bankruptcy may allow you to recover it. The automatic stay requires the creditor to return the vehicle in some jurisdictions. Once the car is sold, recovery is much more difficult.

What is a deficiency balance after repossession?

If your car is repossessed and sold for less than you owe, the remaining balance is called a deficiency. For example, if you owed $15,000 and the car sold for $10,000, the $5,000 deficiency is now unsecured debt. This deficiency is dischargeable in bankruptcy.

How does Rhode Island's vehicle exemption work with repossession?

Rhode Island protects up to $12,000 of equity in your vehicle. If you file Chapter 7, the exemption protects that amount of equity from the bankruptcy trustee. The exemption does not stop a secured creditor from repossessing the vehicle, but the automatic stay does.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 to keep my car?

Chapter 13 is usually better if you want to keep a car you are behind on payments for. It allows you to catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years. Chapter 7 can discharge the debt but may require surrendering the vehicle unless you reaffirm or redeem.

Stop Repossession Today

Check your eligibility and learn about your options to protect your vehicle.

Free Discharge Screener
Keep Your Car Guide

## Bankruptcy Timeline in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/timeline.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Bankruptcy Timeline in Rhode Island

Understanding the bankruptcy timeline helps you plan and reduces anxiety. Here is a detailed look at what happens at each stage when filing in Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Timeline: 3-4 Months

Before Filing: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. This is required and takes about 60-90 minutes. Cost: $15-$50.

Day 1: File Your Petition

Your bankruptcy petition, schedules, and statements are filed with the District of Rhode Island. The automatic stay takes effect immediately. Filing fee: $338.

Days 20-40: 341 Meeting of Creditors

Appear before the bankruptcy trustee for a brief hearing. The trustee reviews your finances and assets. Usually 5-15 minutes. Location: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903

Days 30-60: Creditor Objection Period

Creditors have 60 days after the 341 meeting to object to discharge of specific debts. Objections are rare in most cases.

Before Discharge: Financial Management Course

Complete a second required course (debtor education/financial management). Cost: $15-$50. Must be completed before discharge.

Days 60-90: Discharge Granted

The court enters your discharge order, eliminating your qualifying debts. Your case is closed shortly after.

Chapter 13 Timeline: 3-5 Years

Before Filing: Credit Counseling

Same requirement as Chapter 7. Complete within 180 days before filing.

Day 1: File Petition + Plan

File your petition, schedules, and Chapter 13 repayment plan. Automatic stay takes effect. Filing fee: $313.

Day 30: First Plan Payment

Your first plan payment is due within 30 days of filing, even before the plan is confirmed.

Days 20-40: 341 Meeting

Similar to Chapter 7 but the trustee also reviews your proposed repayment plan.

Days 45-90: Plan Confirmation Hearing

The court reviews and confirms your repayment plan. Creditors may object. The judge determines if the plan meets legal requirements.

Years 1-5: Make Plan Payments

Make monthly payments to the Chapter 13 trustee, who distributes funds to creditors according to your confirmed plan.

End of Plan: Discharge

After completing all plan payments and the financial management course, the court grants your discharge.

Key Filing Requirements

1

Credit Counseling

Required before filing. Approved providers at justice.gov.

2

Means Test

Required for Chapter 7 to determine eligibility based on income.

3

Tax Returns

Must provide tax returns for the last 4 years to the trustee.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does Chapter 7 bankruptcy take in Rhode Island?

Chapter 7 bankruptcy typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing, and discharge is usually granted 60 days after the 341 meeting.

How long does Chapter 13 bankruptcy take in Rhode Island?

Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The plan must be confirmed by the court, typically within 2-3 months of filing. Discharge occurs after all plan payments are completed.

What happens at the 341 meeting of creditors?

The 341 meeting is a brief hearing (usually 5-15 minutes) where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and bankruptcy schedules. It is held at the courthouse in Rhode Island or by phone/video. Creditors may attend but rarely do.

Can my bankruptcy case be dismissed?

Yes. Cases can be dismissed for: failing to complete credit counseling, not filing required documents, not passing the means test (Chapter 7), or failing to make plan payments (Chapter 13). Dismissal removes bankruptcy protection.

When does the automatic stay begin?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your bankruptcy petition is filed with the court. It does not matter whether creditors have been notified yet -- the stay is effective immediately.

Ready to Take the First Step?

Check your discharge eligibility with our free tool.

## The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/341-meeting.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Rhode Island

The 341 meeting is one of the most anxiety-inducing parts of bankruptcy, but it is usually quick and straightforward. Here is what to expect in Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors is a brief hearing required in every bankruptcy case under 11 U.S.C. section 341. Despite its name, creditors rarely attend. The meeting is conducted by the bankruptcy trustee assigned to your case -- not the bankruptcy judge.

In Rhode Island, meetings are held at U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903. Many meetings are now conducted by phone or video conference. The typical meeting lasts 5-15 minutes.

What to Bring

1

Photo ID

Valid driver's license, state ID, or passport

2

SSN Proof

Social Security card, tax return, or W-2 showing your full SSN

3

Your Schedules

Copies of your bankruptcy filing for reference

Common Trustee Questions

The trustee will ask you questions under oath. Most are straightforward yes/no questions to verify the accuracy of your filing:

Did you sign the petition and schedules, and is the information accurate?

Did you list all of your assets -- including bank accounts, vehicles, real estate, and personal property?

Did you list all of your debts?

Have you sold, transferred, or given away any property in the last 2-4 years?

Are you expecting any tax refunds, inheritance, or lawsuit proceeds?

Is anyone holding property for you?

Have you filed all required tax returns?

Have you previously filed for bankruptcy?

Tips: Answer honestly and briefly. If you do not know the answer, say so. Do not guess. Your attorney will be present and can help if needed.

What Happens After

After Chapter 7 Meeting

Trustee may request additional documents

60-day period for creditor objections

If no issues, discharge in about 60 days

Case closes shortly after discharge

After Chapter 13 Meeting

Plan confirmation hearing follows (30-45 days later)

Trustee may request plan modifications

Once confirmed, you make plan payments for 3-5 years

Discharge after completing all payments

Tips for Success

Arrive early. If in person, arrive 15 minutes before your scheduled time.

Dress appropriately. Business casual is fine. You do not need a suit.

Be honest. You are under oath. Lying to the trustee is a federal crime.

Be brief. Answer the question asked and stop. Do not volunteer extra information.

Review your schedules. Read through your filing the night before so the information is fresh.

Relax. The vast majority of 341 meetings are routine and uneventful.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

The 341 meeting (named after section 341 of the Bankruptcy Code) is a required hearing in every bankruptcy case. It is held about 30 days after filing and is conducted by the bankruptcy trustee, not the judge. In Rhode Island, meetings are held at U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903 or by phone/video.

What questions will the trustee ask?

Common questions include: Did you list all your assets and debts? Is the information in your schedules accurate? Have you sold or transferred any property recently? Are you expecting any tax refunds, lawsuits, or inheritances? Did you read and understand the bankruptcy forms you signed?

Do I have to go in person?

In Rhode Island, many 341 meetings are conducted by phone or video since the COVID pandemic. Check with your attorney or the court clerk for the current format. If in person, meetings are held at U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903.

Will creditors attend?

In most consumer cases, creditors do not attend. If a creditor does attend, they can ask you questions under oath. Your attorney will be present to object to improper questions.

What should I bring?

Bring: (1) a valid photo ID (driver's license or passport), (2) proof of Social Security number (SSN card, tax return, or W-2), (3) copies of your bankruptcy schedules, and (4) any documents your attorney has requested.

What happens after the 341 meeting?

After the 341 meeting, the trustee may request additional documents or information. In Chapter 7, creditors have 60 days to object to discharge. In Chapter 13, the confirmation hearing follows. If no issues arise, your case proceeds toward discharge.

Preparing for Bankruptcy?

Learn about the full process and check your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/checklist.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for Rhode Island

Filing bankruptcy requires careful preparation. Use this comprehensive checklist to gather everything you need before filing in Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Income Documents

Last 6 months of pay stubs (all jobs)

Last 2 years of federal tax returns (filed copies)

Last 2 years of Rhode Island state tax returns

Current year income documentation (W-2s, 1099s)

Social Security or disability income statements

Unemployment compensation documentation

Pension or retirement income statements

Rental income documentation

Child support or alimony received

Any other sources of income

Asset Documents

Bank statements (all accounts, last 6 months)

Vehicle titles or registration for all vehicles

Most recent mortgage statement

Home value estimate (Zillow, Redfin, or appraisal)

Retirement account statements (401k, IRA, pension)

Life insurance policies (cash value)

Investment and brokerage account statements

List of personal property with estimated values

Business ownership documents (if applicable)

Any property held by others on your behalf

Debt Documents

List of all creditors with names, addresses, account numbers, and balances

Recent statements for all credit cards

Medical bills and collection notices

Student loan statements

Auto loan statements

Personal loan documentation

Judgments, liens, or garnishment orders

Tax debts (IRS notices, state tax notices)

Past-due utility bills

Payday loan documentation

Any debts from lawsuits or legal disputes

Required Steps Before Filing

1

Credit Counseling

Complete an approved credit counseling course within 180 days before filing. Cost: $15-$50. Available online.

2

Credit Reports

Pull free reports from AnnualCreditReport.com to verify all debts are listed.

3

Means Test

Calculate if you qualify for Chapter 7 based on Rhode Island median income.

Rhode Island Exemption Quick Reference

Key Rhode Island Exemptions

Homestead: $500,000

Vehicle: $12,000

Federal exemptions: Not available (must use state exemptions)

Full Rhode Island exemptions guide →

Filing Fees

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Fee waiver: Available for income below 150% of poverty level

Installments: Court may allow payment in installments

Where to File in Rhode Island

Court: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 380 Westminster St, Providence, RI 02903

Districts: Rhode Island has the District of Rhode Island. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Legal assistance: Rhode Island Legal Services (rils.org) may provide free or reduced-cost help with your filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need to file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

You will need: last 6 months of pay stubs, last 2 years of tax returns, bank statements for all accounts, a list of all debts with account numbers and balances, vehicle titles or registration, mortgage statements, and a list of all property you own with estimated values.

How long does it take to prepare a bankruptcy filing?

Gathering documents and completing the required credit counseling course typically takes 1-4 weeks. The actual filing can be done in a day once everything is prepared.

Do I need a lawyer to file bankruptcy?

While you can file pro se (without a lawyer), bankruptcy is complex. Mistakes can cost you property, result in case dismissal, or lead to debts not being discharged. Rhode Island Legal Services (rils.org) may provide free or low-cost assistance if you cannot afford an attorney.

What is the means test?

The means test compares your household income to Rhode Island's median income. If your income is below the median, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7. If above, additional calculations determine eligibility. The median income varies by household size.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13 filing fee: $313. Attorney fees vary but typically range from $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13. You may be able to pay in installments or qualify for a fee waiver.

Ready to Get Started?

Check your eligibility and learn about your options.

Free Discharge Screener
How to File Guide

## Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/emergency-bankruptcy.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Rhode Island

When foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment is days or even hours away, Rhode Island residents can file an emergency bankruptcy petition to trigger the automatic stay immediately.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is an Emergency Bankruptcy Filing?

An emergency bankruptcy filing -- sometimes called a bare-bones or skeleton filing -- is when you file only the minimum required documents to open a case and activate the automatic stay. You file the rest within 14 days.

Minimum Documents Required

Voluntary Petition -- the two-page form that officially opens the case

Creditor Matrix -- a list of all known creditors with names and addresses

Credit Counseling Certificate -- proof of the required pre-filing credit counseling course (can be completed online in about an hour)

When to Consider Emergency Filing in Rhode Island

Emergency Filing Makes Sense When

A foreclosure sale is scheduled within days

Your employer received a garnishment order that starts next pay period

A creditor is actively repossessing your vehicle

A bank account levy is about to clear

A lawsuit judgment is about to be enforced

Utility shutoff is imminent

Better to File a Complete Petition When

You have several weeks before any deadline

You want to avoid the risk of missing the 14-day supplement deadline

Your financial situation is complex and needs careful preparation

You need time to gather all financial documents

The 14-Day Deadline

After an emergency filing in D.R.I., you have 14 days to file all remaining documents:

Schedules A/B through J (assets, liabilities, income, expenses)

Statement of Financial Affairs

Means Test calculation (Form 122A or 122C)

Tax returns for the most recent year

Pay stubs for the 60 days prior to filing

If you miss the 14-day deadline and do not receive an extension, the court will dismiss your case. This can limit the automatic stay protection in any future filing within one year.

Rhode Island Exemption Quick Reference

When you complete your schedules, you will need to claim exemptions to protect your property:

Homestead: $500,000

Vehicle: $12,000

For a complete list, see the Rhode Island exemptions page.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file an emergency bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. You can file an emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy petition in Rhode Island to get the automatic stay protection immediately. You file only the two-page voluntary petition, a creditor list, and proof of credit counseling. You then have 14 days to file the remaining schedules and documents.

How fast can the automatic stay take effect in Rhode Island?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed with the D.R.I. bankruptcy court. If you file electronically during court hours, it can happen within minutes. Emergency filings are designed for situations where a foreclosure sale, wage garnishment, or repossession is imminent.

What happens if I miss the 14-day deadline?

If you fail to file the remaining schedules and documents within 14 days, the court may dismiss your case. You can request a one-time extension, but it is not guaranteed. A dismissal could limit your ability to refile and get the automatic stay again.

Check Your Filing Eligibility

Before filing -- emergency or otherwise -- make sure you are eligible for a bankruptcy discharge.

## Divorce and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/divorce-and-bankruptcy.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Divorce and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Divorce and bankruptcy often occur together. Understanding how they interact in Rhode Island can help you protect your assets, manage shared debts, and plan the best filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Order: Bankruptcy Before, During, or After Divorce

1

Before Divorce

Filing a joint bankruptcy before divorce can eliminate shared unsecured debt and simplify the divorce property settlement. You save on filing fees by filing one joint case instead of two individual cases.

2

During Divorce

You can file bankruptcy during divorce proceedings. The automatic stay will pause any debt-related orders but does not affect custody, support, or the divorce itself. Coordination between courts is needed.

3

After Divorce

Filing after divorce means you file individually. Your divorce decree does not override bankruptcy law -- a creditor can still come after you for joint debts even if the decree assigns them to your ex-spouse.

What Bankruptcy Cannot Discharge in Divorce

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(5) and (a)(15), the following divorce-related debts are not dischargeable:

Child support -- all arrears and ongoing obligations

Alimony / spousal support -- all arrears and ongoing obligations

Property settlement obligations -- debts to a former spouse under a divorce decree, separation agreement, or property settlement (non-dischargeable in Chapter 7; potentially dischargeable in Chapter 13)

Hold-harmless agreements -- if you agreed to pay a joint debt in the divorce, that obligation survives Chapter 7

Key difference: In Chapter 13, property division debts (section 523(a)(15)) may be dischargeable, while domestic support obligations (section 523(a)(5)) are never dischargeable.

Joint Debts and the Co-Debtor Stay

When one spouse files Chapter 13, the co-debtor stay under 11 U.S.C. section 1301 temporarily protects the non-filing spouse from creditors trying to collect on joint consumer debts. This protection does not exist in Chapter 7.

This is particularly important in Rhode Island where both spouses may be on the mortgage, car loan, or credit cards. The co-debtor stay lasts as long as the Chapter 13 plan is active.

Rhode Island Property Considerations

Homestead exemption: $500,000 -- the bankruptcy and divorce courts both consider home equity

Vehicle exemption: $12,000

See the full Rhode Island exemptions guide for details.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy during a divorce in Rhode Island?

Yes. You can file bankruptcy before, during, or after a divorce in Rhode Island. The timing affects how debts and property are divided. Filing before the divorce can simplify property division by eliminating joint unsecured debt.

Does bankruptcy discharge alimony or child support?

No. Domestic support obligations including alimony and child support are not dischargeable in bankruptcy under any chapter. They are priority debts that must be paid in full.

What happens to joint debts in bankruptcy?

If one spouse files bankruptcy and discharges a joint debt, the other spouse remains fully liable. The creditor can pursue the non-filing spouse for the entire balance. A Chapter 13 co-debtor stay can temporarily protect the non-filing spouse during the repayment plan.

Planning Your Next Step

If you are considering bankruptcy alongside divorce, check your discharge eligibility first.

Discharge Screener

## Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/credit-card-debt.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Credit card debt is one of the most common reasons Rhode Island residents file for bankruptcy. In most cases, credit card balances can be completely eliminated through Chapter 7 or significantly reduced through Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Each Chapter Handles Credit Card Debt

Chapter 7

Credit card debt is eliminated entirely in Chapter 7. The discharge typically comes in 3-4 months. You must pass the means test to qualify. Any non-exempt assets could be liquidated, but most Rhode Island filers keep all their property under the state's exemptions.

Chapter 13

Credit card debt is included in your 3-5 year repayment plan. You pay a percentage of the total based on your disposable income. Many filers pay only 10-30 cents on the dollar. The remaining balance is discharged at the end of the plan.

Recent Charges: The 90-Day and 70-Day Rules

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), certain recent credit card activity is presumed to be non-dischargeable:

Luxury goods/services over $800 charged within 90 days before filing

Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days before filing

These are rebuttable presumptions. The credit card company must file an adversary proceeding to challenge discharge, and you can present evidence that the charges were made with the intent and ability to repay.

Best practice: Stop using credit cards well before filing. Using cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud.

Credit Card Lawsuits and Judgments in Rhode Island

If a credit card company sues you in Rhode Island and obtains a judgment, they can:

Garnish your wages

Levy your bank account

Place a lien on your property

Filing bankruptcy stops all of these through the automatic stay. Pre-existing judgment liens on personal property can often be avoided (removed) under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f) if they impair your Rhode Island exemptions.

Rhode Island Exemptions Relevant to Credit Card Debt

Homestead: $500,000 -- protects home equity from credit card judgment liens

Vehicle: $12,000

Full Rhode Island exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge credit card debt in Rhode Island bankruptcy?

Yes. Credit card debt is unsecured debt and is generally dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Rhode Island. In Chapter 7, it is eliminated entirely. In Chapter 13, you pay a percentage based on your disposable income over 3-5 years, and the rest is discharged.

Will recent credit card charges be discharged?

Charges of over $800 for luxury goods or services within 90 days of filing, and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days of filing, are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). The creditor must object for this to apply.

Can creditors sue me for credit card debt?

Yes. In Rhode Island, credit card companies can sue you, obtain a judgment, and garnish your wages or levy your bank account. Filing bankruptcy stops these actions through the automatic stay.

Ready to Explore Your Options?

Find out if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate your credit card debt.

Discharge Screener
Cost Calculator

## Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/converting-chapters.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Rhode Island

If your financial situation changes after filing, Rhode Island debtors can convert between bankruptcy chapters. Understanding your conversion rights can help you get the best outcome.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Conversion Options

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13

One-time right of conversion under section 706(a)

Common when you have assets at risk of liquidation

Useful when income increases or you want to save property

Must be able to propose a feasible repayment plan

No additional filing fee

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7

Right to convert at any time under section 1307(a)

Not available if case was originally Chapter 7 and converted to 13

Common when income drops and plan payments become unaffordable

Must still pass the means test if required

No additional filing fee

Common Reasons for Conversion in Rhode Island

Job loss or income reduction -- Chapter 13 plan payments become unaffordable, convert to Chapter 7

Income increase -- No longer pass the means test for Chapter 7, convert to Chapter 13

Property at risk -- Chapter 7 trustee identifies non-exempt property, convert to Chapter 13 to protect it

Mortgage arrears -- Need Chapter 13's structured repayment to catch up on missed mortgage payments and keep your home (homestead exemption: $500,000)

Plan failure -- Unable to complete Chapter 13 plan, convert to Chapter 7 for immediate discharge

What Happens When You Convert

File a motion or notice of conversion with the D.R.I. bankruptcy court

New schedules may be required -- particularly if significant time has passed or circumstances have changed

New trustee is assigned -- Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 trustees are different

The automatic stay continues -- no gap in protection

A new 341 meeting may be scheduled -- the new trustee needs to examine you

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I convert my bankruptcy case to a different chapter?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 706 and section 1307, you generally have the right to convert between chapters. Chapter 7 debtors can convert to Chapter 13 once as a matter of right. Chapter 13 debtors can convert to Chapter 7 at any time if they have not previously converted from Chapter 7.

Does converting restart my case?

Converting does not start a completely new case. Your case number stays the same, and the automatic stay remains in effect. However, new schedules and forms may need to be filed, and certain deadlines reset.

Are there fees to convert?

There is no additional filing fee to convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7. Converting from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 requires paying the difference in filing fees ($313 minus $338, so no additional fee). Attorney fees may apply for the additional work.

Not Sure Which Chapter Is Right?

Compare the two main consumer bankruptcy chapters side by side.

Ch. 7 vs 13 Guide

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/chapter-7-eligibility.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Rhode Island

Chapter 7 bankruptcy can eliminate most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Here is how to determine whether you qualify to file in Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Means Test

The primary eligibility requirement for Chapter 7 is the means test under 11 U.S.C. section 707(b). It has two parts:

Step 1: Income Comparison

Compare your current monthly income (averaged over the 6 months before filing) to the Rhode Island median income for your household size. If your income is at or below the median, you automatically pass.

Step 2: Expense Deductions (If Above Median)

If your income exceeds the median, you deduct allowed expenses (IRS standards plus actual secured debt payments) from your income. If the remaining disposable income is below the threshold, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

Tip: Use the means test calculator to check your eligibility before paying for a consultation.

Prior Filing Bars

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9), you cannot receive a Chapter 7 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years (unless you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan)

Use the 1328(f) Screener to check whether prior filings affect your eligibility.

Other Eligibility Requirements

Credit counseling: Must complete an approved course within 180 days before filing

No recent dismissals for cause: If a case was dismissed in the past 180 days for violating a court order or failure to appear, you cannot file immediately

Domicile requirement: You file in the D.R.I. if you have lived in Rhode Island for the greater portion of the past 180 days

No fraud: Fraudulent transfers or concealment of assets can bar discharge under section 727(a)(2)

Tax returns: You must provide your most recent tax return to the trustee at least 7 days before the 341 meeting

Rhode Island Exemption Snapshot

If you qualify for Chapter 7, your exempt property is protected from liquidation:

Homestead: $500,000

Vehicle: $12,000

Complete Rhode Island exemptions list →

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for Chapter 7 in Rhode Island?

To qualify for Chapter 7 in Rhode Island, your household income must be at or below the state median income for your household size. If above median, you must pass the means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the result shows insufficient disposable income, you still qualify. Current Rhode Island median income figures are updated twice yearly.

What disqualifies you from filing Chapter 7?

You may be disqualified from Chapter 7 if: you received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years, a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years, your income is too high to pass the means test, you committed bankruptcy fraud, or you had a case dismissed within 180 days for specific reasons.

Do I have to take a class before filing Chapter 7 in Rhode Island?

Yes. You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. After filing, you must also complete a financial management course before receiving your discharge. Both courses can be done online and typically cost $25-$50 each.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filings and time bars instantly.

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/chapter-13-eligibility.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Rhode Island

Chapter 13 lets Rhode Island residents keep their property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Here are the eligibility requirements.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Core Eligibility Requirements

1. Regular Income

You must have regular income to fund a Chapter 13 repayment plan. Acceptable sources include:

Wages or salary

Self-employment income

Social Security benefits

Pension or retirement income

Rental income

Regular contributions from a spouse or household member

2. Debt Limits

Your total debts (secured plus unsecured) must not exceed $2,750,000. This combined limit was established by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act of 2022.

3. Tax Filing

You must have filed all required federal and state tax returns for the four years before filing.

4. Credit Counseling

You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing.

Plan Length: 3 Years vs. 5 Years

Below Median Income

If your household income is at or below the Rhode Island median, your plan can be as short as 36 months (3 years) and no longer than 60 months (5 years).

Above Median Income

If your household income exceeds the Rhode Island median, your plan must be 60 months (5 years). This is called the applicable commitment period.

Prior Filing Bars for Chapter 13

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f), you cannot receive a Chapter 13 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 4 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 2 years

These time bars only prevent a discharge -- you can still file Chapter 13 to use the automatic stay and structured repayment even without eligibility for a discharge.

Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) Screener →

Chapter 13 Advantages in Rhode Island

Keep your home: Catch up on mortgage arrears while protecting up to $500,000 in equity

Keep your car: Restructure auto loans (potential cramdown if older than 910 days) with $12,000 exemption protection

No means test barrier: Unlike Chapter 7, high earners can file Chapter 13

Co-debtor stay: Protects co-signers on consumer debts during the plan

Broader discharge: Some debts not dischargeable in Chapter 7 may be discharged in Chapter 13

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the debt limits for Chapter 13 in Rhode Island?

As of 2024, the debt limit for Chapter 13 is $2,750,000 in combined secured and unsecured debts. This limit was updated by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act. If your debts exceed this amount, you may need to file Chapter 11 instead.

Do I need regular income to file Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 requires regular income sufficient to fund a repayment plan. This can include wages, salary, commissions, self-employment income, Social Security, pension, rental income, or even consistent support from a spouse or domestic partner.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan in Rhode Island?

If your income is below the Rhode Island median for your household size, your plan can be 3-5 years. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The plan cannot exceed 5 years (60 months).

Check Your Eligibility

See if prior filings affect your Chapter 13 discharge eligibility.

## Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/buying-a-house.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent homeownership. Thousands of Americans buy homes after bankruptcy every year. Here are the waiting periods, requirements, and strategies for Rhode Island residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years from discharge

VA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with lender approval)

USDA Loan3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with approval)

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

The waiting period starts from the discharge date, not the filing date. For Chapter 7, that is typically 3-4 months after filing. For Chapter 13, it is at the end of the 3-5 year plan.

Steps to Prepare for Homeownership

1

Rebuild Credit

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge. Make small purchases and pay in full monthly. Consider a credit-builder loan. Target a 620+ score for FHA, 640+ for conventional.

2

Save for Down Payment

FHA requires 3.5% down with a 580+ score (10% with 500-579). Conventional requires 3-5%. VA and USDA offer zero-down options.

3

Stabilize Employment

Lenders want to see 2 years of stable employment. Consistent income documentation is critical. Self-employed borrowers may need additional tax returns.

Rhode Island Homestead Exemption

When you do buy a home in Rhode Island, the state homestead exemption protects $500,000 in home equity from future creditors. This means:

Your home equity up to $500,000 is protected from judgment creditors

If you ever need to file bankruptcy again, this equity is exempt from the Chapter 7 trustee

Learn more about keeping your house in Rhode Island bankruptcy →

Frequently Asked Questions

How long after bankruptcy can I buy a house in Rhode Island?

After Chapter 7: 2-4 years depending on the loan type (FHA requires 2 years, conventional requires 4 years). After Chapter 13: 1-2 years after discharge (FHA may allow applications during an active plan with trustee approval). VA loans require 2 years after Chapter 7 and may allow applications during Chapter 13.

Can I get a mortgage during Chapter 13?

Some lenders and the FHA allow mortgage applications during an active Chapter 13 plan if you have made 12 consecutive on-time payments and receive court and trustee approval. This is difficult but not impossible.

Will bankruptcy destroy my credit score?

Bankruptcy causes an initial credit score drop of 100-200 points. However, many Rhode Island filers see improvement within 12-18 months because the debt-to-income ratio improves and negative accounts stop accumulating late payments. Active rebuilding efforts accelerate recovery.

Start Planning Your Path to Homeownership

Check your discharge eligibility and learn about rebuilding credit after bankruptcy.

Discharge Screener
Rebuild Credit Guide

Browse Other States

## Rhode Island Bankruptcy Glossary
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/glossary.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Glossary

Key bankruptcy terms and definitions for Rhode Island filers, from automatic stay to wildcard exemption.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Terms A-Z

341 Meeting (Meeting of Creditors)
A required hearing where the bankruptcy trustee (and any creditors who choose to attend) asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and petition. Held about 30 days after filing in D.R.I..

Adversary Proceeding
A lawsuit within a bankruptcy case, often filed to challenge the dischargeability of a specific debt or to recover fraudulent transfers.

Automatic Stay
A court order that takes effect immediately upon filing, stopping most collection actions, lawsuits, foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments. See 11 U.S.C. section 362.

Chapter 7
Liquidation bankruptcy that eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. A trustee may sell non-exempt assets to pay creditors. Requires passing the means test.

Chapter 13
Reorganization bankruptcy for individuals with regular income. Debtor proposes a 3-5 year repayment plan to catch up on secured debts and pay a portion of unsecured debts.

Cramdown
Reducing the amount owed on a secured debt to the current value of the collateral. Available in Chapter 13 for vehicles purchased more than 910 days before filing and other secured debts.

Credit Counseling
A required course from an approved provider that must be completed within 180 days before filing bankruptcy. A second course (debtor education) is required before discharge.

Creditor
Any person or entity to whom you owe money. Includes secured creditors (backed by collateral), unsecured creditors (no collateral), and priority creditors (taxes, support obligations).

Discharge
A court order that permanently eliminates your personal liability for dischargeable debts. The discharge injunction (section 524) prevents creditors from collecting on discharged debts forever.

Disposable Income
Income remaining after subtracting allowed expenses. In Chapter 13, disposable income determines your plan payment amount.

Exemptions
Laws that protect specific property from being taken by the trustee in Chapter 7 or reduce what you pay in Chapter 13. Rhode Island homestead exemption: $500,000; vehicle: $12,000.

Fraudulent Transfer
A transfer of property made to hinder, delay, or defraud creditors. The trustee can recover such transfers made within 2 years before filing (or longer under state law).

Lien
A legal claim on property as security for a debt. Common types include mortgage liens, auto liens, tax liens, and judgment liens.

Lien Stripping
Removing a wholly unsecured junior mortgage in Chapter 13. If the home value is less than the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage can be treated as unsecured debt.

Means Test
A formula under section 707(b) that determines Chapter 7 eligibility based on income and expenses. Compares your income to Rhode Island median income for your household size.

Motion for Relief from Stay
A request by a creditor asking the court to lift the automatic stay, usually to allow foreclosure or repossession to proceed.

Non-Dischargeable Debt
Debts that survive bankruptcy, including student loans (absent undue hardship), most taxes, child support, alimony, debts from fraud, and DUI-related debts.

Petition
The official document filed with the bankruptcy court to start a case. Includes your personal information, chapter selected, and basic financial data.

Plan (Chapter 13)
A proposal showing how you will repay creditors over 3-5 years. Must pay priority debts in full, cure arrears on secured debts, and pay unsecured creditors at least what they would receive in Chapter 7.

Priority Debt
Debts that must be paid in full in Chapter 13 and cannot be discharged in Chapter 7. Includes recent taxes, child support, alimony, and certain government fines.

Proof of Claim
A form filed by a creditor establishing the amount and type of debt owed. Required for the creditor to receive payment in the case.

Reaffirmation Agreement
A voluntary agreement to remain liable for a debt after bankruptcy, typically used to keep a car or other secured property. Must be filed with the court.

Schedules
Detailed forms listing all assets, debts, income, expenses, and recent financial transactions. Required within 14 days of filing.

Secured Debt
A debt backed by collateral (like a mortgage or car loan). The creditor can repossess the collateral if the debt is not paid.

Trustee
A court-appointed official who administers your bankruptcy case. Chapter 7 trustees liquidate non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 trustees collect and distribute plan payments.

Unsecured Debt
A debt not backed by collateral, such as credit cards, medical bills, and personal loans. Generally dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Wildcard Exemption
An exemption that can be applied to any property type. Check Rhode Island exemption statutes for availability.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months through liquidation of non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 is a 3-5 year repayment plan for people with regular income. Chapter 7 has income limits (means test); Chapter 13 has debt limits ($2,750,000).

What does discharge mean in bankruptcy?

A discharge is a permanent court order that eliminates your personal liability for specified debts. After discharge, creditors can never collect on those debts again. Not all debts are dischargeable -- student loans, most taxes, child support, and alimony typically survive.

Learn More About Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Chapter 7 Guide
Chapter 13 Guide

## Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/payday-loans.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Payday loans trap borrowers in cycles of high-interest debt. Bankruptcy can break that cycle by discharging payday loan obligations entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Payday Loans Are Dischargeable

Payday loans are unsecured consumer debt. They are fully dischargeable in bankruptcy -- meaning you will not owe anything after your case is completed.

Chapter 7: Payday loan debt is eliminated entirely in 3-4 months

Chapter 13: Payday loans are included in your repayment plan as general unsecured debt, typically receiving only a fraction of the balance

Stopping ACH Debits and Post-Dated Checks

Many payday lenders require ACH (electronic) authorization or post-dated checks as a condition of the loan. Once you file bankruptcy:

The automatic stay prohibits the lender from processing any further debits

Revoke your ACH authorization in writing with both the lender and your bank

Notify your bank of the bankruptcy filing and request a stop payment on any post-dated checks

If the lender processes a payment after filing, they have violated the stay and may owe you damages

Take action immediately upon filing: contact your bank, revoke ACH authorizations in writing, and keep copies of all communications.

Can Payday Lenders Challenge Discharge?

In rare cases, a payday lender might argue that the loan was obtained through fraud (11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)). To succeed, they would need to prove:

You made a false representation (e.g., lied about income or employment)

You intended to deceive the lender

The lender relied on your misrepresentation

In practice, payday lenders rarely challenge discharge because the amounts are small and the legal costs are high. Taking out a payday loan when you know you plan to file bankruptcy could raise issues, so consult an attorney about timing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge payday loans in bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. Payday loans are unsecured debt and are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Rhode Island. Once discharged, the lender cannot collect, even if they have post-dated checks or ACH authorization.

Can a payday lender cash my check after I file bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents payday lenders from cashing post-dated checks or processing ACH debits after you file. If they do, it is a stay violation and you may be entitled to damages.

Should I stop paying payday loans before filing?

Once you decide to file bankruptcy, continuing to pay payday loans means less money available for necessary living expenses. However, consult an attorney about timing, especially regarding any pending ACH authorizations or post-dated checks.

Break the Payday Loan Cycle

Check if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate payday loan debt.

Discharge Screener
Rhode Island Chapter 7 Guide
