## Judgments and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/judgments.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Judgments and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

A civil judgment against you does not mean you are out of options. Bankruptcy can discharge the underlying debt and even remove judgment liens from your property in Rhode Island.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Discharging Judgments in Bankruptcy

Whether a judgment is dischargeable depends on the type of debt behind it:

Generally Dischargeable

Credit card judgments

Medical debt judgments

Personal loan judgments

Breach of contract judgments

Deficiency judgments (auto, mortgage)

Generally NOT Dischargeable

Judgments based on fraud

Judgments for willful injury

DUI/DWI damage judgments

Domestic support judgments

Student loan judgments (absent hardship)

Government fine/penalty judgments

Removing Judgment Liens Under Section 522(f)

Even when a judgment is discharged, the lien it created on your property may survive unless you take action. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can file a motion to avoid (remove) a judicial lien if it impairs your exemptions.

How It Works in Rhode Island

Home: If your home equity minus the judgment lien and any mortgages is less than the $500,000 homestead exemption, the lien impairs your exemption and can be avoided

Vehicle: Same analysis using the $12,000 vehicle exemption

Other property: Any exempt property with an impairing judgment lien qualifies

You must file a separate motion with the D.R.I. court to avoid the lien. The discharge alone does not remove it.

Stopping Judgment Enforcement

The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately stops all judgment enforcement:

Wage garnishment must stop

Bank account levies are frozen

Property liens cannot be enforced

No new collection lawsuits can be filed

Existing lawsuits are paused

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge a judgment in bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. Most money judgments based on unsecured debt (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) are dischargeable in bankruptcy in Rhode Island. The underlying debt determines dischargeability -- if the original debt was dischargeable, the judgment based on that debt is also dischargeable.

Can I remove a judgment lien from my property?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can avoid (remove) a judicial lien on your property to the extent it impairs your bankruptcy exemptions. In Rhode Island, this is commonly used to remove judgment liens from homes (homestead exemption: $500,000) and vehicles (exemption: $12,000).

Does the judgment creditor have to stop collecting?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection on judgments the instant you file. The creditor cannot garnish wages, levy bank accounts, or enforce the judgment in any way while the stay is in effect.

Facing a Judgment?

Find out if bankruptcy can eliminate your judgment debt.

Discharge Screener

## Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/second-mortgage.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

If your Rhode Island home is worth less than your first mortgage, Chapter 13 lien stripping can eliminate your second mortgage entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is Lien Stripping?

Lien stripping is a powerful tool available in Chapter 13 bankruptcy under 11 U.S.C. section 506(a). It allows you to remove a junior mortgage (second mortgage, HELOC, or other junior lien) from your home when:

Your home's current fair market value is less than the balance on your first mortgage

The second mortgage is therefore wholly unsecured -- there is no equity supporting it

You file a motion with the D.R.I. court asking to strip the lien

Once the Chapter 13 plan is completed, the second mortgage lien is permanently removed from your property.

How It Works

1

Get Home Appraised

Determine your home's fair market value. It must be less than the first mortgage balance for the second mortgage to qualify for stripping.

2

File Lien Strip Motion

Your attorney files a motion to value the collateral and strip the wholly unsecured junior lien. The second mortgage holder can object.

3

Complete the Plan

The second mortgage is treated as unsecured debt in your 3-5 year plan. Upon plan completion and discharge, the lien is permanently removed.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Second Mortgages

Chapter 7

Lien stripping is not available in Chapter 7 per Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015). The personal liability on the second mortgage can be discharged, but the lien survives. The creditor cannot collect from you personally, but the lien remains attached to the property.

Chapter 13

Lien stripping is available. If the home value is below the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage lien is stripped and the debt is treated as unsecured. Upon successful plan completion, the lien is permanently removed from your title.

Rhode Island Homestead Exemption Impact

The Rhode Island homestead exemption of $500,000 protects your home equity from creditors. In a lien stripping scenario, the exemption is less relevant because the home is underwater -- but it becomes critical if property values recover.

Learn more about protecting your home in Rhode Island →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I strip a second mortgage in bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes, through a process called lien stripping in Chapter 13. If your home is worth less than what you owe on your first mortgage, the second mortgage is considered wholly unsecured and can be treated as general unsecured debt in your plan. At completion of the plan, the lien is removed.

Can I strip a second mortgage in Chapter 7?

No. The Supreme Court ruled in Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015) that Chapter 7 debtors cannot strip wholly underwater junior liens. Lien stripping is only available in Chapter 13 (and Chapter 11) in Rhode Island.

What if I have some equity above the first mortgage?

If your home value exceeds the first mortgage balance even slightly, the second mortgage is not wholly unsecured and cannot be stripped. The full second mortgage balance would need to be addressed in your Chapter 13 plan or reaffirmed.

Explore Lien Stripping Options

Learn more about how lien stripping can save your home and reduce your debt.

Lien Stripping Guide
Rhode Island Chapter 13 Guide

## Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/bank-account.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Understanding how bankruptcy interacts with your bank accounts in Rhode Island can help you protect your money and avoid unexpected freezes or seizures.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bank Account Levies and the Automatic Stay

Before bankruptcy, judgment creditors can obtain a court order to levy (freeze and seize) your bank account in Rhode Island. The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay prohibits all new levies.

New levies: Completely blocked by the stay

Pending levies: May need a motion to release frozen funds

Government debts: Tax levies are paused but not permanently blocked by the stay

Bank Setoff Rights

If you owe money to the same bank where your accounts are held (credit card, loan, line of credit), the bank has a right of setoff -- meaning they can take money from your deposit account to cover what you owe, even without a court order.

How to Protect Yourself

Before filing: Open a new account at a bank or credit union where you have no debts

Move direct deposit: Switch your payroll direct deposit to the new bank

Keep records: Do not move large sums that could look like a fraudulent transfer

Timing: Coordinate with your attorney to minimize risk around the filing date

Do not drain accounts or make large transfers without attorney guidance. Transfers of property before filing can be scrutinized as potential fraudulent conveyances.

Protecting Bank Deposits in Rhode Island

In Chapter 7, the trustee can claim non-exempt cash in your bank accounts on the date of filing. In Rhode Island:

Homestead exemption: $500,000 (does not protect cash)

Vehicle exemption: $12,000 (does not protect cash)

Check Rhode Island exemption statutes for any wildcard or cash exemptions that may protect bank deposits

Many Rhode Island filers protect bank deposits by timing the filing when account balances are low (right after paying normal living expenses like rent, utilities, and food).

Rhode Island exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can creditors freeze my bank account in Rhode Island?

Yes. Before bankruptcy, a creditor with a judgment can levy (freeze and seize funds from) your bank account in Rhode Island. After you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately prohibits any new levies and may require release of frozen funds.

Should I open a new bank account before filing?

If you owe money to the same bank where you have checking or savings accounts, the bank may exercise its right of setoff -- taking money from your account to cover the debt. Opening an account at a bank where you have no debts before filing is a common protective measure.

Will bankruptcy affect my bank account?

Filing bankruptcy does not close your bank accounts. However, a Chapter 7 trustee could claim non-exempt funds. In Rhode Island, check which exemptions protect your cash and bank deposits.

Prepare Before You File

Check your discharge eligibility and learn how to protect your assets.

Discharge Screener
Rhode Island Filing Checklist

## Chapter 13 Payments in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/chapter-13-payments.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Chapter 13 Payments in Rhode Island

Your Chapter 13 plan payment is based on your income, expenses, and debts. Here is how it works in Rhode Island and what to do if you cannot keep up.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Payments Are Calculated

Three tests determine your minimum Chapter 13 payment:

1. Disposable Income Test

Your monthly income minus reasonable living expenses (IRS standards and actual secured debt payments). All disposable income must go into the plan.

2. Liquidation Test (Best Interest of Creditors)

Unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get if you filed Chapter 7. If you have significant non-exempt assets, this raises your payment.

3. Secured and Priority Debt Test

Your plan must pay all priority debts in full (taxes, child support arrears) and cure any secured debt arrears (mortgage, car loan). These are non-negotiable minimums.

Plan Length in Rhode Island

Below Median Income

36-60 months. Your plan can be as short as 3 years but cannot exceed 5 years. Shorter plans mean higher monthly payments but less time in bankruptcy.

Above Median Income

60 months (required). If your income exceeds the Rhode Island median for your household size, you must commit to a full 5-year plan.

What Gets Paid in a Chapter 13 Plan

Debt TypeTreatment

Mortgage arrearsPaid in full to cure default and keep home (homestead exemption: $500,000)

Car loanPaid through plan, possible cramdown if older than 910 days

Priority taxesPaid in full over plan length

Child support arrearsPaid in full

Unsecured debtPaid 0-100% depending on disposable income

Trustee feesTypically 5-10% of plan payments, built into payment amount

Missing Payments and Modification

If you fall behind on Chapter 13 payments:

Contact the trustee immediately -- many will work with you on temporary hardship

File a plan modification if the change is permanent (job loss, pay cut, medical issues)

Hardship discharge may be available under section 1328(b) if you have paid the equivalent of what Chapter 7 creditors would have received

Convert to Chapter 7 if you no longer have sufficient income for any plan

Do not simply stop paying. Communicate with the trustee and your attorney to explore all options before your case is dismissed.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much will my Chapter 13 payment be in Rhode Island?

Your monthly payment depends on your disposable income (income minus allowed expenses), the amount of secured and priority debts you must pay, and the liquidation test (unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get in Chapter 7). Most Rhode Island Chapter 13 payments range from a few hundred to a few thousand dollars per month.

What happens if I miss a Chapter 13 payment?

Missing payments can result in the trustee filing a motion to dismiss your case. If your case is dismissed, you lose the protection of the automatic stay and all the progress you have made. Contact the trustee immediately if you are going to be late -- many allow temporary payment modifications.

Can I change my Chapter 13 payment amount?

Yes. You can file a modified plan with the D.R.I. court if your financial situation changes. Common reasons include job loss, pay cut, medical emergency, or increased expenses. The modified plan must still meet all legal requirements.

Explore Your Options

Rhode Island Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy Income Limits in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/income-limits.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Bankruptcy Income Limits in Rhode Island

Your income determines which bankruptcy chapter you qualify for in Rhode Island and how much you pay in Chapter 13. Here is how the income limits work.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Income Limits

Chapter 7 uses the means test to determine eligibility. The test compares your household's current monthly income (averaged over the prior 6 months) to the Rhode Island median income for your household size.

If Below Median Income

You automatically pass the means test and qualify for Chapter 7. No further calculation is needed.

If Above Median Income

You must complete the full means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the remaining disposable income falls below the threshold, you still qualify. The means test uses IRS expense standards plus your actual secured debt payments.

Rhode Island median income figures are updated twice per year (April and October) by the U.S. Trustee Program. Check bankruptcymeanstest.org for current figures.

Chapter 13 -- No Income Ceiling

Chapter 13 has no maximum income limit. Anyone with regular income can file, regardless of how high that income is. However, your income affects:

Plan length: Above-median income requires a 5-year plan; below-median allows 3-5 years

Payment amount: Higher income means higher monthly plan payments

Debt limits: Total debts (secured plus unsecured) cannot exceed $2,750,000

Chapter 13 requires regular income -- wages, salary, self-employment, Social Security, pension, or other consistent sources.

Income Calculation Details

Current monthly income (CMI) includes almost all income from all sources over the 6 months before filing:

Wages, salary, overtime, bonuses, commissions

Self-employment income (gross receipts minus ordinary business expenses)

Interest, dividends, rental income

Pension, retirement, annuity payments

Unemployment compensation

Contributions from household members (spouse, non-filing partner)

Excluded from CMI: Social Security benefits, payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

For Chapter 7, your household income must be at or below the Rhode Island median for your household size, or you must pass the means test expense deduction. Chapter 13 has no income ceiling -- anyone with regular income can file. However, Chapter 13 has a debt limit of $2,750,000.

What if my income is too high for Chapter 7?

If your income exceeds the Rhode Island median, you still might qualify through the means test expense deductions. If not, Chapter 13 allows any income level -- higher income just means higher plan payments over 3-5 years.

Does household size affect income limits?

Yes. The median income threshold increases with household size. A single filer has a lower threshold than a family of four. Household size typically includes everyone living in the home and dependents you support.

Check Your Eligibility

The means test and discharge screener can help determine your options.

Means Test Guide

## Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/disqualifications.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Rhode Island

Not everyone qualifies for bankruptcy relief. Understanding the disqualifications can help Rhode Island residents plan their filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Bars: When You Cannot File

Prior Discharge Time Bars

Prior DischargeNew Chapter 7New Chapter 13

Chapter 78 years4 years

Chapter 136 years*2 years

*The 6-year bar for Ch.13-to-Ch.7 is waived if you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan.

Use the 1328(f) Screener to calculate your exact eligibility dates.

Grounds for Denial of Discharge

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a), the court can deny your Chapter 7 discharge entirely if:

Concealment or transfer of property with intent to defraud within 1 year before filing or during the case (section 727(a)(2))

Destruction or concealment of records relating to your financial condition (section 727(a)(3))

Perjury or false statements in connection with the case (section 727(a)(4))

Failure to explain loss of assets or deficiency of assets (section 727(a)(5))

Failure to complete the post-filing financial management course (section 727(a)(11))

Prior discharge within the applicable time period (section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9))

Credit Counseling Requirement

You cannot file bankruptcy in D.R.I. without first completing a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. There is no exception except:

Exigent circumstances that merit a waiver (very rarely granted)

The approved providers for your district are unable to provide services (almost never happens)

Courses can be completed online, by phone, or in person and typically take about one hour. A second course (debtor education / financial management) is required after filing but before discharge.

180-Day Dismissal Bar

Under 11 U.S.C. section 109(g), you cannot file a new bankruptcy petition if a prior case was dismissed within the past 180 days for:

Willful failure to obey orders of the court

Willful failure to appear before the court

Filing a motion to dismiss after a creditor requested relief from the automatic stay

If you have had a recent dismissal, count the days carefully. Filing before the 180-day period expires can result in immediate dismissal of the new case.

Means Test Disqualification (Chapter 7 Only)

If your income is above the Rhode Island median and you do not pass the means test expense deductions, you cannot file Chapter 7. Your options are:

File Chapter 13 instead (no income ceiling)

Wait until your income drops (e.g., job change, retirement)

Recalculate after 6 months if a high-income period falls out of the lookback window

Frequently Asked Questions

What disqualifies you from filing bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

You may be disqualified from bankruptcy if: you did not complete credit counseling, you received a recent prior discharge (8 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.7, 4 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.13, 6 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.7, 2 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.13), you committed fraud, your prior case was dismissed within 180 days for cause, or your income is too high for Chapter 7 and debts exceed Chapter 13 limits.

Can I be denied a bankruptcy discharge?

Yes. Even if you are allowed to file, the court can deny discharge for cause under section 727(a). Grounds include concealing assets, destroying records, committing perjury, failing to explain loss of assets, or failing to complete the financial management course.

What happens if my bankruptcy was recently dismissed in Rhode Island?

If a case was dismissed within the past 180 days for willful failure to obey a court order, appear before the court, or properly prosecute the case, you cannot file a new case immediately. Additionally, repeat filings within one year can limit or eliminate automatic stay protection.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filing bars and time limits instantly.

## Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Rhode Island?
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/buy-car-chapter-13.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Rhode Island?

Yes -- but you need court permission first. Buying a car during Chapter 13 requires filing a motion, getting trustee approval, and showing the purchase fits your budget.

Quick answer: You can buy a car during Chapter 13 in Rhode Island, but you must file a Motion to Incur New Debt and get court/trustee approval before signing anything. The court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and affordable within your plan.

The Process: Step by Step

1

Get Pre-Approved

Find a lender willing to work with Chapter 13 debtors. Get the terms in writing -- vehicle, price, interest rate, monthly payment.

2

File the Motion

File a Motion to Incur New Debt with the District of Rhode Island bankruptcy court. Include all purchase details and an updated budget.

3

Court Approval

The trustee reviews and may object. If no objection, the court typically grants the motion within 2-3 weeks. Do not sign anything until approved.

What the Court Wants to See

Necessity: Is the vehicle necessary for work, medical appointments, or family needs? Luxury vehicles are almost always denied.

Affordability: Can you make both the car payment AND your Chapter 13 plan payment? The court will review your current budget.

Reasonable terms: Interest rates above 20-25% may be questioned. The court wants to see you are not being exploited.

Vehicle value: A used car in the $5,000-$15,000 range is typical. Requests for new or expensive vehicles face heavier scrutiny.

Down payment source: The court may ask where the down payment is coming from. It should not come from plan payments or exempt funds earmarked for creditors.

Do NOT sign a purchase agreement, make a deposit, or take possession of a vehicle before the court grants your motion. Unauthorized debt during Chapter 13 can result in case dismissal.

Interest Rates and Lender Options

Borrowers in active Chapter 13 cases face limited lender options and higher interest rates. Typical options in Rhode Island include:

Credit unions: Often the best rates for Chapter 13 borrowers. Some Rhode Island credit unions specifically work with bankruptcy filers.

Subprime auto lenders: Companies like Capital One Auto Finance, Westlake Financial, and DriveTime specialize in lending to borrowers in bankruptcy. Rates range from 12-24%.

Buy-here-pay-here dealers: Available but typically the worst terms. Interest rates can exceed 25%, and vehicle quality is often poor.

Section 506 cramdown: If your current vehicle loan is more than 910 days old, you may be able to reduce the balance to the vehicle's value through your Chapter 13 plan rather than buying a new car.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I buy a car while in Chapter 13 in Rhode Island?

Yes, but you need court approval. You must file a motion with the bankruptcy court requesting permission to incur new debt. The trustee and court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary, whether you can afford it within your plan, and whether the terms are reasonable.

Do I need trustee approval to buy a car in Rhode Island Chapter 13?

Yes. In Rhode Island, as in all states, you must get trustee approval before taking on new debt during Chapter 13. The trustee will review the purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and whether it fits within your budget and plan obligations.

What interest rate can I expect when buying a car during Chapter 13?

Interest rates for borrowers in Chapter 13 are typically higher -- often 15-25% from subprime lenders. Some credit unions and specialized lenders work with Chapter 13 debtors at better rates. The court may reject a purchase with an unreasonably high interest rate.

How do I file a motion to buy a car in Chapter 13?

You or your attorney file a Motion to Incur New Debt with the bankruptcy court. The motion should include the vehicle details, purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and an updated budget showing you can afford both the car payment and your plan payment.

Can I lease a car during Chapter 13 in Rhode Island?

Leasing during Chapter 13 is treated the same as buying -- you need court approval. Some courts view leases less favorably because they do not build equity. The total lease cost will be compared against purchasing.

What happens if I buy a car without court approval?

Taking on new debt without court approval is a violation of your Chapter 13 plan. The trustee may file a motion to dismiss your case or convert it to Chapter 7. The unauthorized debt could also be treated as fraudulent.

Related Topics

Chapter 13
Exemptions

## Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Rhode Island?
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/eviction.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Rhode Island?

Timing is everything. Bankruptcy can halt an eviction if you file before a judgment for possession is entered. After a judgment, the protection is limited.

Quick answer: If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy immediately stops the eviction through the automatic stay. If a judgment already exists, section 362(b)(22) allows the landlord to proceed. File before the judgment to maximize protection.

Before vs. After Eviction Judgment

Before Judgment

Automatic stay stops eviction

Landlord cannot proceed in state court

Chapter 13 can cure back rent over 3-5 years

You must pay post-petition rent going forward

Emergency filing possible in hours

After Judgment

Section 362(b)(22) limits protection

Landlord can proceed with eviction

Exception: if state law allows cure after judgment, you may file certification under 362(l)

Most Rhode Island courts apply this strictly

Chapter 7 will discharge the rent debt but not stop eviction

Rhode Island Eviction Timeline

Governing law: R.I. Gen. Laws 34-18-35 et seq.

Rhode Island requires a 5-day notice for nonpayment. After filing, a hearing is typically scheduled within 5-10 days in District Court. The process usually takes 2-4 weeks total.

Understanding this timeline is critical for deciding when to file bankruptcy. Once the landlord obtains a judgment for possession, your bankruptcy protections are significantly reduced.

Key window: File bankruptcy BEFORE the judge enters a judgment for possession. Once a judgment is entered, section 362(b)(22) may allow the eviction to continue despite your filing.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Evictions

Chapter 7

Discharges back rent (pre-petition)

Automatic stay is temporary (case lasts 3-4 months)

Does not allow you to cure arrears

Best if you plan to move anyway

Eliminates personal liability for lease balance

Chapter 13

Allows curing back rent through plan

Automatic stay lasts 3-5 years (plan duration)

You must assume the lease and stay current

Best if you want to keep your housing

Plan payments include arrears catch-up

Emergency Filing to Stop Eviction

If eviction is imminent, an emergency bankruptcy filing can be done quickly:

Bare-bones petition: File just the petition, Statement of Social Security Number, and credit counseling certificate. You have 14 days to file the remaining schedules.

Immediate protection: The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the bankruptcy court -- not when the landlord is notified.

Proof of filing: Get a copy of the filed petition with the case number and give it to your landlord and the state court handling the eviction.

Filing fee: Chapter 7 costs $338 and Chapter 13 costs $313. Fee waivers are available for those below 150% of the poverty line.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop an eviction in Rhode Island?

It depends on timing. If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts eviction proceedings. If a judgment for possession already exists, section 362(b)(22) limits the stay's protection, and the landlord may be able to proceed.

What is section 362(b)(22)?

Section 362(b)(22) of the Bankruptcy Code is an exception to the automatic stay. If a landlord obtained a judgment for possession before you filed bankruptcy, the stay does not prevent the landlord from continuing the eviction. This was added by the 2005 BAPCPA amendments to prevent abuse of bankruptcy to delay evictions.

What if I file bankruptcy before the eviction judgment in Rhode Island?

If no judgment for possession has been entered, the automatic stay stops the eviction cold. The landlord cannot proceed with the eviction, change locks, or remove your property. However, you must still pay post-petition rent -- the stay does not excuse future rent payments.

Can I cure a lease default in Chapter 13?

In Chapter 13, you can cure lease defaults (back rent) through your repayment plan. You assume the lease and pay arrears over the plan period (3-5 years). This only works if your landlord has not yet obtained a judgment for possession.

Does filing bankruptcy erase back rent?

Chapter 7 can discharge back rent owed before filing, but it does not stop an eviction if a judgment already exists. Chapter 13 lets you cure arrears while keeping your home. Post-petition rent must always be paid regardless of the chapter filed.

How quickly can I file bankruptcy to stop an eviction in Rhode Island?

Emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy filings can be done in hours. You file the petition with minimal schedules and have 14 days to complete the rest. The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the court.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

Garnishment

## Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/car-lease.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

You have two options: assume the lease (keep the car) or reject it (return the car). The 60-day clock starts when you file.

Quick answer: When you file bankruptcy in Rhode Island, you must decide within 60 days whether to keep or surrender your leased vehicle. If you keep it, you must stay current. If you surrender it, remaining lease obligations become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Assume vs. Reject: Your Two Options

Assume (Keep the Car)

Continue making lease payments as normal

Cure any past-due payments

Lease continues as if bankruptcy never happened

Must not be a financial burden on your budget

Must decide within 60 days of filing

Good option if payments are affordable and you need the car

Reject (Return the Car)

Surrender the vehicle to the leasing company

Early termination fees become unsecured debt

Remaining lease payments become unsecured debt

Deficiency balance discharged in Chapter 7

Paid at reduced rate in Chapter 13 plan

Good option if payments are too high or car is not needed

The 60-Day Deadline

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you have 60 days from the date of filing to assume or reject your car lease in Chapter 7. This deadline can be extended by the court for cause, but extensions are not automatic.

If you fail to act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. This means you lose the vehicle and the remaining obligations become unsecured debt.

Mark your calendar: 60 days from your bankruptcy filing date is your deadline for the lease decision. Missing it means automatic rejection.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Lease Treatment

Chapter 7

60-day assume/reject deadline applies

Rejected lease: deficiency balance discharged

Assumed lease: continues normally

No option to modify lease terms

Best if you want a clean break from a bad lease

Chapter 13

Decision made as part of your plan

Can cure past-due payments through plan

Rejected lease: deficiency paid through plan at reduced rate

More flexibility on timing

Best if you are behind on payments but want to keep the car

Practical Considerations

Mileage and wear: If you are over your mileage limit or the car has excess wear, rejecting the lease in bankruptcy eliminates those penalties. They become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Gap insurance: If the car is worth less than the payoff, gap insurance may cover the difference. Bankruptcy rejection does not trigger gap insurance -- it covers total loss/theft only.

New lease after bankruptcy: You can lease a new car after bankruptcy, but expect higher deposits and potentially higher rates. Some Rhode Island dealers work with recent bankruptcy filers.

Automatic stay protection: While your case is pending, the leasing company cannot repossess the vehicle. If you plan to reject, you have time to arrange alternative transportation.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens to my car lease if I file bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

In both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13, you must decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease within 60 days of filing. If you assume, you keep the car and continue making payments. If you reject, you return the vehicle and any remaining balance is treated as unsecured debt.

What does it mean to assume a lease in bankruptcy?

Assuming a lease means you agree to keep the lease, stay current on all payments, and cure any past-due amounts. The lease continues as if bankruptcy was never filed. You must be current on payments and the vehicle must not be a financial burden.

What does it mean to reject a lease in bankruptcy?

Rejecting a lease means surrendering the vehicle to the lessor. Any early termination fees, remaining payments, or deficiency balance become unsecured debt that can be discharged in Chapter 7 or paid through a Chapter 13 plan at pennies on the dollar.

What is the 60-day deadline for lease decisions?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you must assume or reject an unexpired lease within 60 days of filing (for Chapter 7). If you do not act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. In Chapter 13, you can assume or reject as part of your plan.

Can I get out of a bad car lease through bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. Rejecting a car lease in bankruptcy is one of the cleanest ways to exit a bad lease. Early termination fees and remaining balance become unsecured debt. In Chapter 7, this debt is typically discharged entirely.

Will the leasing company repossess my car during bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents the leasing company from repossessing the vehicle once you file. However, you must make your decision within 60 days and stay current on payments if you want to keep the car.

Related Topics

Chapter 13

## Timeshares and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/timeshare.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Timeshares and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Trapped in a timeshare? Bankruptcy may be the most reliable exit. You can surrender the timeshare and discharge the mortgage, maintenance fees, and assessments.

Quick answer: You can surrender a timeshare in Chapter 7 bankruptcy and discharge all associated debts -- mortgage, maintenance fees, assessments, and special levies. This is often faster and cheaper than timeshare exit companies.

Chapter 7: The Clean Exit

Chapter 7 is the most straightforward way to exit a timeshare:

List the timeshare as property on Schedule A/B (real or personal property, depending on how the timeshare is structured)

Indicate surrender on your Statement of Intention (Schedule D/Form 108)

Discharge the debt: The mortgage balance, maintenance fees, assessments, and any other obligations are discharged

Deed transfer: The timeshare company typically handles transferring the deed out of your name after discharge

Important: You are responsible for maintenance fees that accrue between filing and the date the timeshare is actually transferred. Work with the trustee to expedite the surrender.

Chapter 13: Keep or Surrender

Chapter 13 gives you more options:

Surrender through plan: Include the timeshare surrender in your Chapter 13 plan. Pre-petition arrears are treated as unsecured debt, paid at the plan percentage (often 10-30 cents on the dollar).

Keep and pay: If you want to keep the timeshare, continue paying the mortgage and fees. Include any arrears in the plan.

Lien stripping: If the timeshare is worth less than what you owe, you may be able to strip the lien in some circumstances -- though this is more common with second mortgages on primary residences.

What Debts Are Covered?

Timeshare mortgage: Discharged in Chapter 7, paid through plan in Chapter 13

Maintenance fees (past due): Discharged as unsecured debt

Special assessments: Discharged as unsecured debt

Exchange program fees: Discharged (RCI, Interval International, etc.)

Collection agency debts: If the timeshare company sold the debt, the buyer's claim is also discharged

Bankruptcy vs. Timeshare Exit Companies

Bankruptcy

Filing fee: $338 (Chapter 7)

Legally binding discharge

Eliminates ALL timeshare debts

Process takes 3-4 months

Court-supervised, transparent

Also eliminates other qualifying debts

Exit Companies

Typical cost: $3,000-$10,000+

No legal guarantee of results

High rate of failure/scams

Process can take 12-24 months

No regulatory oversight

Only addresses the timeshare

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I get rid of a timeshare through bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Yes. In Chapter 7, you can surrender a timeshare and discharge the remaining obligations -- mortgage balance, maintenance fees, and assessments. The timeshare interest is listed as property of the estate and abandoned or surrendered through the bankruptcy process.

Will bankruptcy stop timeshare maintenance fees?

Bankruptcy discharges your personal liability for pre-petition maintenance fees. However, maintenance fees that accrue after filing are your responsibility until the timeshare is actually transferred out of your name. Surrendering in bankruptcy accelerates that process.

What happens to a timeshare mortgage in bankruptcy?

A timeshare mortgage is treated like any other secured debt. In Chapter 7, you can surrender the timeshare and discharge the debt. In Chapter 13, you can either keep paying it or surrender it through the plan. The lender may still foreclose on the timeshare interest itself.

Can I keep my timeshare in Rhode Island bankruptcy?

Yes. If you want to keep the timeshare, you can reaffirm the debt (Chapter 7) or continue paying through your plan (Chapter 13). You must stay current on mortgage payments, maintenance fees, and assessments.

Are timeshare exit companies legitimate?

Many timeshare exit companies charge thousands of dollars and deliver no results. Bankruptcy is often a more reliable and less expensive way to exit a timeshare. A Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 -- far less than most exit company fees.

Does the timeshare HOA get paid in bankruptcy?

Pre-petition HOA/maintenance fees are unsecured debt and typically receive little or nothing in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, they are paid at the same rate as other unsecured creditors -- often pennies on the dollar.

## Can I Travel During Chapter 13 in Rhode Island?
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/travel-chapter-13.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Can I Travel During Chapter 13 in Rhode Island?

Domestic travel is fine. International travel may require notifying your trustee. The real issue is not where you go -- it is what you spend.

Quick answer: Domestic travel during Chapter 13 in Rhode Island is unrestricted. For international travel, notify your trustee as a precaution. The key rule: do not spend money on luxury travel while your creditors are being paid less than they are owed.

Domestic vs. International Travel

Domestic Travel

No restrictions or permissions needed

No notification to trustee required

Keep spending reasonable

Budget travel is generally fine

Work travel is never an issue

International Travel

No legal prohibition, but notify your trustee

Some trustees want advance notice

Extended trips may raise questions

Keep records of purpose and expenses

Luxury international travel is risky

What Could Cause Problems

Falling behind on plan payments: If you travel and then miss a plan payment, the trustee will connect the dots. This is the fastest way to get your case dismissed.

Lavish spending: Posting photos from expensive resorts while paying creditors 10 cents on the dollar creates a credibility problem. Trustees and creditors can review social media.

Unexplained income: If you are traveling frequently on a budget that says you have no discretionary income, expect questions about undisclosed income or assets.

Extended international trips: Spending months abroad can suggest you have income or assets not disclosed in your schedules.

The safest approach: keep travel modest, stay current on plan payments, and notify your trustee before any international travel.

What Is Always Fine

Work travel: Required by your employer, fully reimbursed -- no issue whatsoever

Family emergencies: Travel for funerals, medical emergencies, or family crises

Local/regional day trips: Taking the kids to a state park or visiting family

Budget vacations: A modest road trip or camping trip within your means

Medical travel: Traveling for medical treatment or appointments

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I travel during Chapter 13 in Rhode Island?

Yes. Domestic travel is generally not restricted during Chapter 13 in Rhode Island or any other state. International travel may require notifying your trustee, depending on local practice. The key concern is whether travel spending conflicts with your budget and plan obligations.

Do I need court permission to travel during Chapter 13?

For domestic travel, no court permission is needed. For international travel, some trustees and courts want advance notice. There is no statute requiring permission, but your plan requires you to devote all disposable income to creditors -- luxury travel could raise issues.

Can the trustee stop me from traveling?

The trustee cannot directly stop you from traveling. However, if you spend money on non-essential travel and then fall behind on plan payments, the trustee can file a motion to dismiss your case. Lavish travel spending can also trigger a motion to modify your plan upward.

Is international travel allowed during Chapter 13?

There is no law prohibiting international travel during Chapter 13. However, best practice is to notify your trustee before international trips. Some trustees view international travel as a sign that you have more disposable income than reported.

What about work travel during Chapter 13?

Business travel is not an issue. If your employer requires travel, that is a legitimate expense. Keep records showing the travel was work-related, especially if questioned by the trustee.

Can I take a vacation during Chapter 13?

Modest vacations are generally acceptable. The concern is extravagant spending while creditors are receiving less than what they are owed. A reasonable road trip or modest vacation is unlikely to cause problems. A cruise or international resort vacation may attract scrutiny.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

## What Bills to Stop Paying Before Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/stop-paying-bills.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

What Bills to Stop Paying Before Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Not all debts are created equal. Knowing which bills to stop paying -- and which to keep paying -- can save you thousands before you file.

Quick answer: Stop paying unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) that will be discharged. Keep paying secured debts you want to keep (mortgage, car loan) and essential services (utilities, insurance). Do not make large payments to any single creditor in the 90 days before filing.

Bills You Can Stop Paying

Credit cards: All credit card debt is dischargeable. Stop paying, but stop using the cards too. New charges near filing can be challenged as fraud.

Medical bills: Medical debt is unsecured and fully dischargeable. Collections calls are annoying but temporary.

Personal loans: Unsecured personal loans from banks, online lenders, and finance companies are dischargeable.

Old utility bills: Past-due amounts on closed accounts are dischargeable. (Keep current utilities paid -- see below.)

Payday loans: Dischargeable unsecured debt. Payday lenders may threaten criminal action but cannot follow through on threats related to civil debt.

Collection accounts: Debts already in collections are dischargeable. The collection agency loses its right to collect once you file.

Bills You Should Keep Paying

Mortgage (if keeping the house): Falling behind creates arrears that complicate your case. In Chapter 13, you must be current on post-petition payments.

Car loan (if keeping the car): Same logic. Stay current on vehicles you need for work and daily life.

Current utilities: Electricity, water, gas, internet. Utility companies can require a deposit after bankruptcy but cannot shut off service solely because you filed.

Insurance: Auto insurance is legally required in Rhode Island. Health insurance protects against new medical debt.

Child support/alimony: These are not dischargeable and must be paid. Falling behind creates a priority debt that must be paid in full in Chapter 13.

Taxes: Recent income taxes (within 3 years) are priority debts. Older tax debts may be dischargeable under specific conditions.

The 90-Day Preference Period

Under 11 U.S.C. section 547, the bankruptcy trustee can claw back payments made to creditors during the 90 days before filing if they exceed $600 to a single creditor. For payments to insiders (family, business partners), the look-back period is one year.

Common mistake: Paying off a credit card or repaying a family loan right before filing. The trustee can sue the recipient to recover that money for the benefit of all creditors. This is called a preferential transfer.

$600+ to one creditor in 90 days -- potentially recoverable

Any amount to family in 12 months -- potentially recoverable

Regular, ordinary payments (mortgage, car loan, utilities) -- generally safe

Frequently Asked Questions

What bills should I stop paying before bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Generally, you should continue paying secured debts you want to keep (mortgage, car loan) and essential bills (utilities, insurance). You can typically stop paying unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) that will be discharged in bankruptcy. Consult with a bankruptcy attorney before making changes.

Should I stop paying credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Yes, in most cases. Credit card debt is unsecured and will be discharged in bankruptcy. Continuing to pay credit cards before filing wastes money that could go toward filing costs or essential expenses. However, stop making new charges on the cards -- using credit cards with no intent to repay is fraud.

What is the preference period in bankruptcy?

The preference period is 90 days before filing (1 year for payments to insiders like family members). Payments of $600 or more to a single creditor during this period can be clawed back by the trustee as preferential transfers. Paying a favorite creditor right before filing can backfire.

Should I keep paying my mortgage before bankruptcy?

If you want to keep your house, yes -- keep paying the mortgage. If you plan to surrender the house, you can stop paying. In Chapter 13, mortgage arrears can be cured through the plan, but you must stay current on post-petition payments.

Should I keep paying my car loan before bankruptcy?

If you want to keep the vehicle, continue making payments. If you plan to surrender it, you can stop. In Chapter 13, you can cure arrears through the plan and may be able to reduce the loan balance to the vehicle's value (cramdown) if the loan is more than 910 days old.

Can creditors sue me if I stop paying before filing?

Yes, creditors can file lawsuits and obtain judgments. However, once you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay halts all collection activity. If you plan to file within 30-60 days, the risk of a completed lawsuit is low. A judgment lien, however, can complicate your bankruptcy.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/credit-cards-before-filing.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Stop using credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Charges made near filing can be challenged as fraud -- and the presumption works against you.

Quick answer: Do not use credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are presumed fraudulent. These debts may survive your discharge.

The Two Presumption Periods

90-Day Rule: Luxury Goods

Applies to purchases of luxury goods/services

Threshold: $800+ to a single creditor

Within 90 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(I)

70-Day Rule: Cash Advances

Applies to cash advances

Threshold: $1,100+ in aggregate

Within 70 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(II)

What You Should Do

Stop Using Cards Immediately

The moment you decide to file bankruptcy, stop all credit card use. Here is why:

Every charge creates risk. Even necessary purchases can be challenged if made close to filing.

Intent matters. Using a credit card when you know you will not repay it is the definition of fraud in this context.

The burden shifts to you. Within the 90/70 day windows, YOU must prove the charges were not fraudulent. Outside those windows, the creditor must prove fraud.

What Charges Are Safe?

Groceries and essential food -- not luxury, reasonably necessary

Gas for work commute -- necessary for income

Medical expenses -- necessary for health

Basic utilities -- if auto-billed to the card, switch to bank payment

Even "safe" charges should be avoided if possible. Use cash or debit instead. The cleaner your pre-filing credit card activity, the smoother your case.

What Happens If Charges Are Challenged

If a credit card company believes you ran up charges with no intent to repay, they can file an adversary proceeding -- a lawsuit within your bankruptcy case. The process:

Creditor files a complaint alleging non-dischargeability under section 523(a)(2)

You must respond within 30 days

Discovery phase: both sides exchange evidence (statements, purchase records)

Trial or settlement: most adversary proceedings settle before trial

If the creditor wins, those specific charges survive your discharge

Adversary proceedings are expensive and time-consuming for both sides. Creditors typically only file them for significant amounts ($2,000+). But avoiding the risk entirely is the smartest strategy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use credit cards before filing bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Technically you can, but it is strongly discouraged. Charges made with no intent to repay can be challenged as fraud. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days of filing are presumed fraudulent. Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are also presumed fraudulent.

What is the 90-day rule for credit cards before bankruptcy?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), charges for luxury goods or services exceeding $800 made within 90 days of filing bankruptcy are presumed to be non-dischargeable. The creditor does not have to prove fraud -- you must prove the charges were not fraudulent.

What is the 70-day rule for cash advances?

Cash advances exceeding $1,100 taken within 70 days of filing are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). This includes balance transfers, convenience checks, and ATM cash advances.

What counts as a luxury purchase?

Luxury goods and services are items not reasonably necessary for support or maintenance. Groceries, gas, medical expenses, and basic clothing are not luxury. Electronics, jewelry, vacations, expensive dining, and entertainment are typically considered luxury.

What happens if I used credit cards right before filing?

The credit card company may file an adversary proceeding arguing the charges are non-dischargeable due to fraud. If the charges are within the 90/70 day presumption periods, you bear the burden of proving they were not fraudulent. Charges outside those windows require the creditor to prove actual fraud.

Should I close my credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Do not close accounts before filing -- it does not help and can look like you are trying to hide something. Simply stop using the cards. They will be listed in your bankruptcy schedules and the issuers will close them after receiving notice of your filing.

Related Topics

Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

## How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/find-bankruptcy-filing.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Rhode Island

Bankruptcy filings are public records. Here is how to search them in Rhode Island -- from free options to the official PACER system.

Quick answer: Search PACER at pacer.uscourts.gov ($0.10/page, $3 cap per doc). For free access, use CourtListener.com (RECAP archive) or call the District of Rhode Island clerk's office. The Rhode Island bankruptcy court website is https://www.rib.uscourts.gov.

Option 1: PACER (Official System)

PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) is the federal court system's official electronic records platform.

Website: pacer.uscourts.gov

Cost: $0.10 per page, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly charges under $30 are waived.

Search by: Name, case number, Social Security number (last 4), date range

Rhode Island court: https://ecf.rib.uscourts.gov

Registration: Free to create an account. Takes about 5 minutes.

1

Register

Create a free PACER account at pacer.uscourts.gov

2

Search

Use the national case locator or go directly to the Rhode Island bankruptcy court

3

View

View docket entries and download documents ($0.10/page)

Option 2: RECAP / CourtListener (Free)

RECAP is a free browser extension that contributes PACER documents to a public archive. CourtListener hosts this archive and makes it searchable.

Website: courtlistener.com

Cost: Completely free

Coverage: Millions of documents, growing daily. Not every document is available, but many are.

Browser extension: Install RECAP for Chrome or Firefox. When you view PACER documents, RECAP automatically saves a copy to the public archive.

Option 3: Court Clerk's Office

You can contact the District of Rhode Island bankruptcy court clerk's office directly:

Phone: Call the clerk and request a case search by name or case number. Basic case information (filing date, chapter, status) is typically provided for free.

In person: Visit the courthouse and use the public access terminals. No PACER account needed.

Website: https://www.rib.uscourts.gov may have a free case search or CM/ECF lookup

What Information Is Available

Docket sheet: Every document filed in the case, with dates and descriptions

Petition: Debtor's name, address, chapter filed, attorney

Schedules: Assets, liabilities, income, expenses (with sensitive info redacted)

Claims register: Every creditor claim filed in the case

Orders: Court orders, discharge, dismissal

Meeting of creditors (341): Date and outcome

Sensitive information is redacted: full Social Security numbers, complete financial account numbers, and the names of minor children are not visible in public filings.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find bankruptcy filings in Rhode Island?

Bankruptcy filings in Rhode Island are public records available through PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) at pacer.uscourts.gov. You can search by name, case number, or Social Security number. The District of Rhode Island has its electronic filing system at https://ecf.rib.uscourts.gov.

Is PACER free?

PACER charges $0.10 per page viewed, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly fees under $30 are waived. You can also use RECAP (a free browser extension) to access previously downloaded documents at no cost via CourtListener.com.

Can I search bankruptcy filings for free?

Yes. CourtListener.com (via the RECAP archive) has millions of free bankruptcy documents. Many court websites also offer free case information lookups. The bankruptcy court clerk's office will search records for free over the phone for basic information.

Which courts handle bankruptcy in Rhode Island?

Bankruptcy cases in Rhode Island are filed in the District of Rhode Island. The court website is https://www.rib.uscourts.gov. PACER electronic filing is at https://ecf.rib.uscourts.gov.

How long are bankruptcy records available?

Bankruptcy records on PACER are generally available for the life of the case plus several years. Older cases may be archived but still accessible. Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7) from the filing date.

Can I see someone else's bankruptcy filing?

Yes. Bankruptcy filings are public records. Anyone can search PACER or visit the court clerk to view case documents. Some sensitive information (Social Security numbers, financial account numbers) is redacted from public filings.

## Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Rhode Island
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/repo-credit-report.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Bankruptcy does not erase the repo from your credit report -- but it eliminates the debt. Here is what changes, what stays, and how to fix errors.

Quick answer: The repossession entry stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. Bankruptcy discharges the remaining balance (deficiency), which should be reported as $0. If the creditor still reports a balance after discharge, dispute it under the FCRA.

What Changes After Discharge

What Bankruptcy Fixes

Deficiency balance discharged -- you owe $0

Collection activity must stop permanently

Account status should update to "discharged"

No more calls, letters, or lawsuits about the balance

Creditor cannot report a balance owed

What Bankruptcy Does Not Fix

Repossession entry remains on credit report

Late payment history leading up to repo remains

7-year reporting clock does not reset

Bankruptcy filing itself appears on credit report

Credit score impact from both events

Disputing Credit Report Errors

After bankruptcy discharge, review your credit reports from all three bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) for these common errors:

Balance still showing: Should be $0 after discharge. This is the most common error.

"Past due" status: Should show "discharged in bankruptcy" or "included in bankruptcy."

Active collection: The account should not show as in active collection after discharge.

Incorrect dates: The date of first delinquency determines when the entry falls off. Verify this date is correct.

1

Pull Reports

Get free reports from annualcreditreport.com (all three bureaus)

2

Identify Errors

Compare each repo account against your discharge order

3

Dispute

File disputes online with each bureau. Include your discharge order as evidence.

FCRA Rights After Bankruptcy

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) gives you powerful tools to correct your credit report after bankruptcy:

Dispute right: You can dispute any inaccurate information. The bureau has 30 days to investigate and correct or remove the entry.

Creditor obligations: Creditors who report to credit bureaus must update accounts to reflect the discharge. Failure to do so violates the FCRA.

Damages: If a creditor willfully reports incorrect information after discharge, you may be entitled to statutory damages ($100-$1,000), actual damages, and attorney fees under the FCRA.

Discharge injunction: Reporting a balance owed on a discharged debt may also violate the bankruptcy discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524), which can result in contempt sanctions.

Rebuilding Credit After Repo + Bankruptcy

Secured credit card: Start with a secured card ($200-$500 deposit). Use it for small purchases and pay in full monthly.

Credit-builder loan: Some Rhode Island credit unions offer loans specifically designed to rebuild credit.

Authorized user: Ask a trusted person to add you as an authorized user on their account.

On-time payments: Every on-time payment builds your score. Payment history is the largest factor (35%).

Time: The repo and bankruptcy impact your score less as they age. Most people see significant score improvement within 2-3 years of discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does bankruptcy remove a repossession from my credit report in Rhode Island?

No. Bankruptcy does not remove the repossession entry from your credit report. The repo stays on your report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. However, the bankruptcy discharge eliminates the deficiency balance -- the amount you still owe after the repo sale.

What happens to the repo balance after bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the vehicle sold for) is discharged. In Chapter 13, it is treated as unsecured debt and paid at the plan percentage, often cents on the dollar. Either way, the balance should be reported as $0 after discharge.

How should a repo appear on my credit report after discharge?

After bankruptcy discharge, the repossession account should show: balance $0, 'included in bankruptcy' or 'discharged in bankruptcy.' If the report still shows a balance owed, that is a reporting error you can dispute.

Can I dispute a repo on my credit report after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under the FCRA (Fair Credit Reporting Act), you can dispute inaccurate information. If the repo account shows a balance after discharge, shows as 'past due' rather than 'discharged,' or has incorrect dates, file disputes with all three bureaus.

How long does a repo stay on my credit report in Rhode Island?

A repossession stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency, regardless of bankruptcy. The bankruptcy filing itself stays for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7). These are federal rules that apply uniformly across all states including Rhode Island.

Can the repo lender collect after bankruptcy discharge?

No. The discharge injunction under 11 U.S.C. section 524 permanently prohibits the creditor from collecting the discharged debt. If they attempt collection (calls, letters, reporting a balance), you may have a claim for violation of the discharge injunction.

## Can I Keep My Jewelry in Rhode Island Bankruptcy?
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/engagement-ring.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeProvidenceChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Can I Keep My Jewelry in Rhode Island Bankruptcy?

Your engagement ring, wedding band, and other jewelry may be protected by Rhode Island exemptions. Here is what the law says and how values are determined.

Quick answer: Rhode Island is one of the most generous states for jewelry exemptions. Under R.I. Gen. Laws 9-26-4(8), jewelry is exempt without a specific dollar limit. Courts interpret this broadly to protect reasonable personal jewelry. Rhode Island does not allow federal exemptions, but the state exemption is often more favorable.

Rhode Island Jewelry Exemption Details

Rhode Island is one of the most generous states for jewelry exemptions. Under R.I. Gen. Laws 9-26-4(8), jewelry is exempt without a specific dollar limit. Courts interpret this broadly to protect reasonable personal jewelry. Rhode Island does not allow federal exemptions, but the state exemption is often more favorable.

The exemption amount is based on the jewelry's replacement value -- what a comparable item would cost at retail, not what a pawnshop or jeweler would pay you. In practice, most everyday jewelry (wedding rings, watches, earrings) falls well within the exemption limits.

How Jewelry Is Valued

Replacement value standard: The Bankruptcy Code uses replacement value -- the price a retail buyer would pay for a comparable item in similar condition.

Not purchase price: What you paid is irrelevant. A ring purchased for $5,000 may have a replacement value of $2,000-$3,000.

Not resale value: Pawn shop or "we buy gold" prices are lower than replacement value and are not the correct standard.

Professional appraisal: If you have valuable jewelry, get a written appraisal from a certified appraiser. The appraiser should provide a replacement value for insurance purposes.

Trustee assessment: The Chapter 7 trustee may request an appraisal if they believe jewelry values are understated. Most trustees do not pursue jewelry worth less than $1,000-$2,000 above exemption limits.

Practical Reality: Trustees and Jewelry

In practice, bankruptcy trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless its value significantly exceeds the exemption amount. Here is why:

Transaction costs: Selling jewelry at auction or through consignment involves fees that reduce the net recovery for creditors.

Low recovery rates: Used jewelry typically sells for 20-40% of retail replacement value at liquidation.

De minimis recovery: If the net benefit to creditors is less than a few hundred dollars, most trustees determine the sale is not worth the effort.

Emotional factor: Trustees understand that taking wedding rings creates bad optics and may exercise discretion.

Important: Always disclose all jewelry honestly on your schedules. Hiding assets is fraud. If your jewelry is within the exemption, the trustee will not take it. If it is close to the limit, the trustee will likely not pursue it.

Protecting Jewelry That Exceeds the Exemption

If your jewelry exceeds Rhode Island's exemption, you may have options:

Wildcard exemption: If Rhode Island offers a wildcard (general property) exemption, you can stack it on top of the jewelry exemption to protect additional value.

Chapter 13: In Chapter 13, you keep all your property. Instead of surrendering jewelry, you pay creditors the equivalent value through your plan. This is often the better option for people with valuable jewelry.

Negotiate with trustee: You may be able to pay the trustee the non-exempt value in cash to keep the jewelry -- called "buying back" the asset from the estate.

Federal exemptions: If Rhode Island allows the federal exemptions (11 U.S.C. 522(d)), you may get a higher jewelry exemption ($1,875) plus a significant wildcard ($1,475 + up to $13,950 of unused homestead).

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my engagement ring in Rhode Island bankruptcy?

It depends on the ring's value and Rhode Island's exemptions. No dollar limit on jewelry (R.I. Gen. Laws 9-26-4(8)) -- very generous. If your ring's value is below the exemption limit, you can keep it. If it exceeds the limit, you may need to use additional exemptions (like a wildcard) to protect it.

What is the jewelry exemption in Rhode Island?

No dollar limit on jewelry (R.I. Gen. Laws 9-26-4(8)) -- very generous. This applies to all jewelry including engagement rings, wedding bands, watches, and other personal adornment. Values are based on resale/liquidation value, not what you paid.

How is jewelry valued in bankruptcy?

Jewelry is valued at replacement value -- what it would cost to buy a comparable item at retail, not what a jeweler would pay you. In practice, bankruptcy trustees often accept values significantly below retail. A professional appraisal can help establish the value.

What if my jewelry is worth more than the exemption?

If your jewelry exceeds the exemption limit, you may be able to protect the excess using a wildcard exemption (if available in your state). Otherwise, the trustee may seek to sell the jewelry and return the exempt portion to you. In practice, trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless the value significantly exceeds exemptions.

Do I have to disclose all jewelry in bankruptcy?

Yes. All assets must be disclosed on Schedule A/B, including jewelry. Failure to disclose assets is bankruptcy fraud and can result in denial of discharge, fines, or criminal prosecution. Always disclose -- even if you believe the item is fully exempt.

What about inherited jewelry or family heirlooms?

Inherited jewelry and heirlooms are valued the same way as any other jewelry -- replacement value. Sentimental value does not factor into the financial analysis. However, trustees rarely pursue items with modest resale value, even if sentimental value is high.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

## Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Rhode Island Bankruptcy?
Source: https://rhodeislandbankruptcy.org/retirement-accounts.html

Rhode Island Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

Your retirement savings are among the most protected assets in bankruptcy. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law, and IRAs are protected up to $1,512,350. Here is what Rhode Island filers need to know.

Quick answer: Your 401(k), 403(b), pension, and other ERISA-qualified retirement plans are 100% exempt in bankruptcy -- no dollar limit. IRAs (traditional and Roth) are protected up to $1,512,350. Do NOT cash out retirement to pay debts or avoid filing. That converts protected money into unprotected money.

ERISA Plans: 100% Protected

Under federal law (11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(C) and ERISA section 206(d)), employer-sponsored retirement plans are completely exempt from the bankruptcy estate. This includes:

401(k) plans -- no dollar limit on exemption

403(b) plans (tax-sheltered annuities) -- fully exempt

Defined benefit pension plans -- fully exempt

Profit-sharing plans -- fully exempt

457(b) government plans -- fully exempt

Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) -- fully exempt for federal employees

This protection applies nationwide regardless of state exemption choices. The trustee cannot touch these accounts.

IRAs: Protected Up to $1,512,350

Traditional IRAs and Roth IRAs are protected under 11 U.S.C. section 522(n) up to a combined limit of $1,512,350 (adjusted for inflation). This limit applies to:

Traditional IRAs

Roth IRAs

SEP-IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection if employer-sponsored)

SIMPLE IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection)

Rollover amounts from an ERISA plan into an IRA retain their unlimited ERISA protection -- they do not count against the $1,512,350 cap.

Rhode Island-Specific Retirement Protections

Rhode Island does not allow federal exemptions. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law. Rhode Island exempts retirement accounts under R.I. Gen. Laws 9-26-4(11) -- this covers IRAs, 401(k)s, 403(b)s, and other retirement plans to the extent reasonably necessary for support. Rhode Island ERS (Employees' Retirement System) and MERS (Municipal Employees) benefits are fully exempt. The generous $500,000 homestead exemption makes Rhode Island one of the more debtor-friendly states.

Important: Rhode Island uses state only (no federal option) exemptions. If you have a choice between state and federal exemptions, compare the total exemption picture -- retirement is just one factor.

The Biggest Mistake: Cashing Out Retirement

Every year, thousands of people cash out retirement accounts to pay debts or avoid bankruptcy. This is almost always a mistake because:

You lose the exemption. Money in a retirement account is protected. Money in a bank account is not (or minimally protected).

You owe income taxes on the entire withdrawal amount.

You pay a 10% early withdrawal penalty if under age 59 1/2.

The debts may be dischargeable anyway. Credit card debt, medical debt, and personal loans are wiped out in bankruptcy -- you could have kept the retirement money and discharged the debt.

You cannot replace the money. Annual contribution limits mean it takes years to rebuild what you withdrew.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my 401(k) in Rhode Island bankruptcy?

Yes. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law. Your 401(k) is completely protected regardless of balance.

Are IRAs protected?

Yes, up to $1,512,350 combined for all traditional and Roth IRAs. Rollover amounts from ERISA plans keep their unlimited protection.

Can I contribute to retirement during Chapter 13?

Generally yes at your existing level. Increasing contributions during the plan may be challenged by the trustee as reducing disposable income.

What about inherited IRAs?

Inherited IRAs are NOT protected in bankruptcy (Clark v. Rameker, 2014). Only IRAs you funded yourself are exempt. Spousal inherited IRAs may be protected if rolled into your own IRA.

Should I cash out retirement to avoid bankruptcy?

Almost never. You lose the exemption, owe taxes, pay penalties, and the debt might have been dischargeable. Talk to a bankruptcy attorney before touching retirement accounts.

Can creditors garnish retirement accounts?

ERISA plans are generally protected from garnishment. Exceptions: IRS tax levies, divorce QDROs, and certain federal criminal penalties.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt
